question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when their office will announce the awardees of the Central Good Relations Fund 2026-2027.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Northern Ireland Investment Fund.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work ongoing at the Ebrington site.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her work on Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence presents a significant opportunity to improve productivity, create good jobs and strengthen our competitiveness, but it must be adopted in a way that is responsible and inclusive.Last week, I convened the first meeting of my AI Advisory Panel, bringing together expertise from industry, academia, and government. The purpose of the Panel will be to advise me on the potential of AI technologies to deliver transformative economic impact, aligned with my economic priorities. Initially its focus will be to advise on the response to the Matrix ‘AI and the Future of Work’ report, prioritising recommendations and providing guidance on delivery approaches. Alongside this, my Department continues to support businesses and workers through the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre, Invest NI’s digital adoption support, and a strong skills pipeline – from postgraduate AI scholarships to programmes such as Skill Up, Assured Skills Academies and Higher Level Apprenticeships. Within my own Department, AI is being introduced in a controlled and ethical way to support staff and improve efficiency, aligned with wider NICS governance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any eligibility criteria which currently apply to the main housing grants administered by his Department, including Disabled Facilities Grants, Home Improvement Grants and any other schemes available to homeowners.
answer_text: The Housing Executive administers a range of grant schemes on behalf of my Department. The eligibility criteria for each scheme are set out in the table below:SchemeEligibility Criteria Affordable Warmth SchemeProvides financial assistance to improve the thermal efficiency of homes occupied by lowincome households.Available to owneroccupiers and private tenants where the gross annual household income is below £23,000, and the property is the applicant’s main residence.Not available to social housing tenants, holiday homes, bed and breakfast accommodation or commercial premises.Disabled Facilities GrantFunds essential adaptations recommended by an Occupational Therapist to enable a disabled person to live safely and independently.Available to private tenants and homeowners.A means test applies to adult applicants, and grants of up to £35,000 or up to £70,000 in exceptional circumstances may be awarded.Repair GrantSupports the repair or replacement of items following the issue of a statutory notice by a local council, such as a Clean Neighbourhood an Environment Notice or a Notice of Disrepair. Eligible applicants include landlords, agents, some private tenants, and owners or landlords of vacant properties. The maximum grant is £7,500 over a threeyear period, with the level of support determined by the property’s Net Annual Valuation (NAV) and relevant rules. Grant percentages range from 25% to 100% depending on the NAV and tenancy type.Home Repair Assistance Grants*Eligibility includes applicants aged over 60, adults in receipt of incomerelated benefits, or those receiving disabilityrelated benefits.Provides up to £5,000 over three years for minor repairs.Replacement and Renovation Grants*Available where a property is assessed as unfit for habitation. Funding levels range from 50% to 90%, depending on applicant type and location, and are subject to means testing.		*Due to budgetary constraints, these discretionary grants are currently only available in exceptional circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the Northern Ireland Investment Fund.
answer_text: The Investment Fund has provided £178.5 million in support, which in turn has leveraged a further £175 million in additional investment.  This has helped to finance projects across a range of sectors and locations, supporting around an estimated 5,200 jobs as well as developing 15.9 hectares of brownfield land.  There is also a healthy pipeline of projects currently under consideration.In my view, the Investment Fund continues to make a very worthwhile contribution in terms of economic and social regeneration, while providing a vehicle for the effective use of Financial Transactions Capital by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on Meenan Square.
answer_text: Meenan Square is an integral part of the Derry~Londonderry Urban Village area.This transformational capital project is designed to invest in people and places, helping to transform neighbourhoods.Enabling works commenced at the site on 2 February 2026, and the response from the local community has been very positive.   The works are expected to take four to six weeks, after which the main contractor will take possession of the site to begin substantive construction in Spring 2026.Delivered by the Executive Office’s Urban Villages Initiative in partnership with Apex Housing Association and Meenan Square Developments Ltd, the scheme will redevelop the derelict site into a shared mixed-use development, including new housing, a community hub, and essential amenities.It shows what can be achieved when government, housing providers and communities work together.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A2 Buncrana Road Improvement Scheme.
answer_text: Major road schemes are an important part of the work delivered by my Department to improve road safety, reduce journey times and increase reliability across the network.The A2 Buncrana Road is a key strategic route in the North West, providing an important link between Derry and County Donegal, and I recognise the strength of local interest in this scheme.The scheme is being considered as part of the North West Transport Plan, which is intended to provide a longterm, evidencebased framework for future transport investment in the region. Public engagement to inform that Plan has already taken place and will help shape future decisions on major schemes, including the A2.My officials are working collaboratively with Derry City and Strabane District Council and other stakeholders on its development. The draft Vision and Objectives of the Plan have been agreed. My officials and design team partners are currently at a technical stage in the plan process, identifying and assessing a long list of transport options for the plan area in line with agreed vision and objectives. Although the Plan is still in its early stages, engagement with Council officials will continue as the plan advances. As Members will appreciate, my Department continues to operate in a very challenging financial environment, and any decision to recommence work on the A2 Buncrana Road can only be taken when there is sufficient budget certainty and when it is affordable and deliverable alongside other regional priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her decision to progress a study on raising the parapet railings on the Foyle Bridge as a suicide prevention measure.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of developing suicide prevention measures at high risk locations on our infrastructure and have met with the Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O’Neill to discuss the issue and the work my department can do to make a positive contribution to suicide prevention.As part of my Department’s ongoing commitment to explore options for progressing suicide prevention on Foyle Bridge, I allocated £150,000 in December to allow the structural assessment to commence this financial year. My Department’s Partner Consultant has now been commissioned and work has started on collating and reviewing the necessary construction and historical strengthening information for the structure.This information, combined with physical inspections and testing will allow a structural assessment model to be created which will inform whether the bridge can sustain the additional wind loading, due to a heightened parapet.Through the Protect Life 2 Strategy my Department is committed to working with other Agencies and Key Stakeholders to improve safety at high risk locations such as the Foyle Bridge and the Westlink overbridges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 34444/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the North West Transport Plan 2035.
answer_text: The North West Transport Plan is part of a suite of Transport Plans that my department is currently developing in parallel with Council Local Development Plans. This plan will set the framework for making local transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035 and aims to ensure that the transport network meets the needs of the people and businesses living, working and visiting Derry City and Strabane, both now and into the future.The North West Transport Plan provides most detail in Derry and Strabane but will also consider local transport issues across the district.  My department is working closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council and other key stakeholders to develop the North West Transport Plan. Officials are currently identifying and assessing a long list of multi modal transport options for the plan in line with the agreed vision and objectives.   As part of the long-listing stage, we will be considering the A2 Buncrana Road and assessing how any future design might complement other transport network measures being considered, whilst aligning with the objectives of the North West Transport Plan.The identification and assessment of the transport options is underpinned by robust data evidence and a bespoke north west transport model. This evidence will help to inform the development of the plan to ensure a safe, sustainable, and integrated transport network for the North West.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on plans to promote the use of the Ebrington site in order to maximise footfall.
answer_text: Between September 2024 and September 2025, Ebrington welcomed over one hundred thousand people to the site.  The recent Halloween activities, including the funfair, brought in more than forty thousand additional visitors.   Ebrington is home to many businesses and organisations, and has hosted a range of community, music, sporting and charity events, all of which have brought footfall to the area. The Department has invested in a vibrant, varied and inclusive anchor events programme to showcase the site and attract more local visitors and those from further afield.Looking ahead, the new landmark visitor attraction, the Derry~Londonderry North Atlantic (DNA) Museum will open in 2027, further boosting the economic, social, tourism and cultural impact of Ebrington.  By its third year, the museum is expected to attract over seventy-five thousand visitors annually, with TEO contributing £3.3m to this new regional visitor attraction.The Department continues to collaborate with tenants and stakeholders to promote Ebrington ensuring it contributes by creating opportunities, attracting investment and supporting a city-wide approach to music, cultural and other creative events which can showcase Derry~Londonderry and the North West.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her Department’s support for the City of Derry Airport.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is doing to support families with the cost of glasses for children with myopia.
answer_text: Families in Northern Ireland can access General Ophthalmic Services (GOS) vouchers to help cover the cost of standard prescription glasses for children with myopia. Although not mandated in contract, the high majority of General Ophthalmic Services (GOS) contractor practices currently offer optical appliances to correct myopia within the GOS Voucher value. These appliances are nowadays aesthetically pleasing and are clinically fit for purpose. The scheme does not currently extend to specialised myopia control lenses due to insufficient supporting clinical evidence. These lenses must therefore be provided in a private capacity. Officials from the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within my Department are engaging with local clinical academic researchers and health economists to develop a National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) grant application. This work is intended to build an evidence base around the safety, efficacy and outcome measures associated with myopia control interventions, and the findings will help inform future clinical and policy decisions, including whether such interventions could be supported through the GOS voucher scheme in the future.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail why waiting times in Altnagelvin Area Hospital Emergency Department, were over three times the average of other Emergency Departments on 12 October 2025.
answer_text: My Department monitors waiting times in Emergency Departments (EDs) against the 4-hour standard, which measures the percentage of patients either treated, discharged, or admitted within four hours of arrival; and the 12-hour standard, which tracks the number of patients who remain in the ED for longer than 12 hours. This approach provides a more meaningful picture of patient flow and system pressures than average wait times alone.On 12 October 2025, ED attendances at Altnagelvin were slightly higher than forecast numbers. Performance against the 4-hour standard when compared to the previous four Sundays was slightly lower and there were slightly more waits in excess of 12-hours when compared to previous weeks. The Regional Co-ordination Centre (RCC) has responsibility for management of operational pressures on hospital sites. They have advised that they had no concerns with the time to triage in Altnagelvin on 12 October and they were consistently performing well against the 15-minute standard throughout the day. They reported that on the morning of 12 October and through to early afternoon that the time to clinician at Altnagelvin was poor, but was comparable to the other major sites including Antrim and the Royal Victoria Hospital. Altnagelvin was not an outlier in that regard. By mid-afternoon the time to clinician had improved significantly and Altnagelvin was performing better than the other major sites.Among the five largest Type 1 EDs in Northern Ireland, Altnagelvin Hospital has recorded the lowest number of 12-hour breaches so far in 2025/26.  It has also seen a comparatively high proportion of patients assessed and treated within 4 hours, higher than other EDs.  However, the ED at Altnagelvin continues to face significant challenges.  My Department has taken a number of steps to support improvements in patient flow at the hospital, working closely with the Western Trust to address capacity pressures and enhance service delivery.An Enhanced Minor Injuries Unit opened in March 2024 to provide a pathway for patients as an alternative to attend the ED.  The Ambulatory Care Unit was expanded with eight trolleys to provide up to 12,000 annual appointments, alongside a consultant-led Respiratory Hub. The Respiratory Hub will be expanded ahead of this winter to run seven days per week.A Control Room and Discharge Co-ordination team have been established to improve patient flow and support earlier discharges.  Work to increase GP referrals to Hospital at Home and collaboration with care homes are helping to avoid hospital admissions, where appropriate.My Department has provided repurposed funding, as part of winter planning arrangements, for additional dementia and nursing beds; funding for eight short-stay beds and 24/7 ambulance offload cover.  Eight new community beds are planned and three newly qualified ED consultants will be appointed. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times at each Emergency Department, in each week from September 2024 to September 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline why the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital has one of the longest average waiting times amongst all emergency departments.
answer_text: Concern about emergency department waiting times is understandable. My Department monitors emergency department waiting times using the nationally recognised four and 12-hour standards.  These metrics help assess patient flow and the pressures facing emergency services. Among the five largest emergency departments in Northern Ireland, Altnagelvin Hospital has recorded the lowest number of 12-hour breaches so far in 2025/26.  It has also seen a comparatively high proportion of patients assessed and treated within 4 hours, compared with other emergency departments.  However, the emergency department at Altnagelvin continues to face significant challenges.  My Department has taken a number of steps to support improvements in patient flow at the hospital, working closely with the Western Trust to address capacity pressures and enhance service delivery.An enhanced minor injuries unit opened in March 2024 to provide a pathway for patients as an alternative to attend the emergency department.  The ambulatory care unit was expanded, with eight trolleys to provide up to 12,000 annual appointments, alongside a consultant-led respiratory hub which will be expanded to run seven days a week this winter.A control room and discharge coordination team have been established to improve patient flow and support earlier discharges.  Work to increase GP referrals to Hospital at Home and collaboration with care homes are helping to avoid hospital admissions, where appropriate.My Department has provided funding for additional dementia and nursing beds; funding for eight short-stay beds; and, 24/7 ambulance offload cover. Eight new community beds are planned and three newly qualified emergency department consultants will be appointed.  The appointment of these consultants will raise staffing levels to meet GIRFT standards and enhance evening and weekend cover.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is undertaking to support homeowners impacted by Japanese knotweed.
answer_text: Japanese knotweed was recently listed by the EU as a Species of Union Concern, and as such, in Northern Ireland, it is regulated by The Invasive Alien Species (Enforcement and Permitting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2019 (IAS Order).	It is important to highlight that, in both national and EU legislation, my Department’s policy and responsibility towards all Invasive Alien Species, including Japanese Knotweed, is to prioritise the protection of the ecology of an area, as opposed to safeguarding private property or other commercial assets.	When informed of the presence of Japanese knotweed on a landholding, NIEA officials will inform the landowner of their responsibilities, including penalties for non-compliance with the IAS Order.  It is responsibility of landowners to ensure that Japanese knotweed on their property neither expands nor spreads outside of their property boundaries.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will deliver their findings on the level of ADHD services by November 2025.
answer_text: My Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025.   Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any financial support schemes his Department (i) currently offer (ii) are considering, to support homeowners over 55 to remain living in their own home.
answer_text: (i) My Department does not offer any financial support schemes specifically to support homeowners over the age of 55 to remain living in their own home. It does however, through the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, offer a range of private sector grants for homeowners including the statutory Repair Grant and Disabled Facilities Grant as well as three discretionary Home Improvement Grants that people over the age of 55 may be able to avail of:The Repair grant can help landlords where a council has issued a ‘statutory notice’ about repairs on their property. Owners or landlords of a vacant property can apply for this grant to repair or replace existing items.The Renovation Grant is for homes that have been inspected and found to be unfit to live in. The property must be the owner’s main or only home. The Replacement grant is available where a home is unfit to live in and repair is not an option.Due to budget pressures the discretionary grants are currently only awarded in exceptional circumstances and are subject to budget availability within the overall pot of home improvement grant funding provided to the Housing Executive (which also covers statutory grants).(ii) At present my Department is undertaking a review of private sector grants, however I have no plans to implement a scheme specifically to support homeowners over the age of 55.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what contingency arrangements are in place if a water pollution issue were to be identified in the River Faughan.
answer_text: The NIEA Incident Hotline on 0800 80 70 60 and the emergency-pollution@daera-ni.gov.uk email address allow the general public and professional partners to report water pollution incidents on a 24/7, 365 days per year basis. NIEA investigates all reports of water pollution and, following investigation, incidents are classified as having had a High, Medium or Low Severity environmental impact. If a water pollution issue is reported in the River Faughan, the NIEA response will be based on the evidence initially available in respect of the likely environmental impact. NIEA also provides a Continuous Response Officer for the Mobuoy site who will organise any necessary emergency works to minimise any impacts to the River Faughan. Additionally, NIEA aims to inform Northern Ireland Water Ltd where water pollution incidents could potentially impact their abstraction points.NIEA’s initial priority will be to stop the polluting discharge at source and, where possible, mitigate the risk to the receiving waterway.  In parallel with the response, all incidents are investigated as potential criminal offences. Formal enforcement action is normally considered for incidents having a High or Medium Severity environmental impact where a polluter has been identified.  NIEA is committed to tackling water pollution by pursuing offenders in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy under legislation including the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 and the Nutrient Action Programme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2019. In addition to formal prosecution, other enforcement actions available to the Department include the issuing of Warning Letters, Notices and Breaches of Cross-Compliance for agricultural incidents.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27588/22-27, (i) what criteria his Department use to determine the penalty for those who cause pollution; and (ii) whether there are plans to impose tougher penalties on those responsible for pollution.
answer_text: The criteria used to consider the action taken in relation to water pollution were outlined in my response to AQW 27588/22-27. Where a prosecution is taken forward, it is for the court to decide on the appropriate level of fine imposed on those found guilty of a water pollution offence. Breaches applied to farm businesses responsible for water pollution, under the current Cross-Compliance processes, attract a financial penalty which is applied to all payments paid or due to be paid to the farm business in the year of the finding. The matrices used for fixing payment reductions for negligent or intentional Cross-Compliance breaches are listed on the DAERA website. Other enforcement actions available to the Department include the issuing of Warning Letters and formal Notices. My Department’s Enforcement Liaison Group has engaged with the Fisheries Bill team who are reviewing enforcement across a range of policy proposals to inform the draft Fisheries Bill. This includes proposals for the introduction of Fixed Penalty Notices and civil sanctions for non-compliance with certain offences and the increase in some maximum penalties including those for water pollution.  My Department is engaged with officials from the Department of Justice on this issue and the policy consultation will take place later this month.Work is also ongoing to introduce new Farm Sustainability Standards, which will replace the Cross-Compliance penalty regime from 2026.   A new penalty matrix will focus on the severity and impact of any breach and will result in a payment reduction which both acts as a deterrent and is proportionate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27588/22-27, to detail (i) the cost of addressing this pollution; (ii) how much of this cost was paid by the offending companies or individuals; and (iii) the cost to public funds.
answer_text: NIEA does not retain full details of the costs incurred in investigating individual water pollution incidents, however, it has a policy of recovering all reasonable costs from those responsible for causing water pollution incidents that have had a High or Medium Severity environmental impact. Recoverable costs include the costs of investigation, analytical costs and, where appropriate, costs where pollution response consumables have been used.Regarding the High and Medium Severity water pollution incidents investigated by NIEA, referenced in my reply to AQW 27588/22-27, a total of £2084.79 was recovered from polluters. One incident remains ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27588/22-27, what measures have been put in place by these companies to prevent a recurrence of pollution.
answer_text: A key objective of NIEA’s water pollution response and enforcement activities is that any further environmental damage is prevented, and actions are taken by polluters to minimise the risk of recurrence. Financial penalties, whether imposed by the Courts following a criminal conviction, or via the application of a breach of Cross Compliance, where an incident has been sourced to a farm premises, act as a deterrent to recurrence.My Department’s Enforcement Policy outlines the considerations taken into account when progressing enforcement action. This includes consideration of the previous history of the defendant and any previous offences. Where there is a history of offending, formal action is more likely to be initiated.Importantly, my officials continue to work with stakeholders and the public to educate and promote responsible behaviours that will reduce pollution and improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) his Department’s policies on reforestation; and (ii) any actions taken to advance these in the last twelve months.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for forestry policy which aims to deliver the sustainable management of existing woods and forests, and a steady expansion of tree cover to increase the many diverse benefits that forests provide.Within the last 12 months Forest Service has secured independent assurance that its management of the Department’s forests meets the requirements of the UK Forestry Standard, confirming sustainable forest management. Forest Service has also regulated the harvesting and reestablishment of non state woodlands during the period in line with the Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010, which incorporates the requirements of the UK Forestry Standard and sustainable woodland management.In 2024/25, Forest Service has supported through grant aid, 427 hectares of new woodland planting and 75 hectares added to its estate. My Department has also developed a draft Climate Action Plan, that the Executive has agreed to be consulted on in the near future, which includes afforestation targets to be achieved by 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage uptake for the Healthy Start Scheme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Data for the Healthy Start scheme is regularly published by the NHS Business Services Authority, as administrators of the scheme on behalf of England, Wales and Northern Ireland. You can access the most up to data at https://www.healthystart.nhs.uk/healthcare-professionals/ .  The Northern Ireland scheme uptake figures are unavailable by constituency as they are categorised by council area, using postcode data from the Office of National Statistics. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 26152 22-27, to provide the requested detail on the plans outlined in his response.
answer_text: As part of the implementation phase of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy work is being taken forward to develop the first short-term action plan, overseen by a cross-Departmental Project Board.  It is anticipated that the action plan will be published in the coming months. I know that tackling long term vacant properties and creating opportunities for town or city-centre living plays an important role in regenerating towns and cities, supporting places to be vibrant and sustainable, where people want to live, work and visit.  For example, my Department is currently providing funding through an Urban Development Grant of £915,000 to Perry Street in Dungannon. Perry Street was previously a vacant street of derelict and underutilised buildings, and through the support of my Department, there will be nine townhouses, four apartments and three commercial units developed.The Department’s Empty to Occupied scheme will bring seven vacant properties back into use and includes a mixture of commercial and residential units, including thirteen residential apartments.My Department has also commissioned research from the Architectural Heritage Fund on routes to regenerate historic buildings in NI towns for a range of functions but with housing and mixed uses at their core.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many (i) firms; and (ii) individuals, have been (a) fined; and (b) had action taken against them, for pollution in rivers and lakes in the Foyle constituency in the last five years.
answer_text: NIEA seeks to undertake enforcement action that is proportionate to the significance of the offence. This includes consideration of the level of environmental harm, the impact on communities, the level of financial benefit arising from the offence(s), or the regulatory costs avoided. Formal enforcement action is normally considered for incidents having a High or Medium Severity environmental impact where a polluter has been identified. During the 2020-2024 period, of the 13 High or Medium Severity incidents confirmed in the Foyle Constituency, there were 9 where a polluter was identified and NIEA were the lead investigatory agency. In respect of 3 incidents, no polluter was identified and Loughs Agency, who have a remit to investigate water pollution incidents in the Foyle and Carlingford catchments, led on 1 case as recorded on NIEA’s database.Formal enforcement action has been against the following pollution sources:Industry – Four Incidents Three Warning Letters issued and one enforcement case ongoingFarm Businesses – Three IncidentsOne warning letter and breach of Cross Compliance in relation to two linked water pollution incidents and one further Warning Letter issued.Domestic – One IncidentAdvisory Letter issuedNI Water Ltd – One IncidentNo further action as cause of incident not due to the act or default of NI Water Ltd
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to address the waiting times of up to ten hours for patients at the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital, which is far in excess of waiting times in other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust recognises that the ‘Time to Clinician’ in the Altnagelvin Emergency Department (ED) has fallen below standard and they offer their sincere apologies for this and give their assurance that the Trust is working hard to ensure that no patients experience lengthy waits to be seen by a clinician.My Department has asked the Trust to provide assurance that all steps are being taken to address delays urgently.  The Trust has advised that they have put in place a number of measures including a focus by Site Co-ordination with time to clinician discussed as a safety Key Performance Indicator (KPI).  The Regional Coordination Centre (RCC) continue to maintain close communication with the Trust across 7 days to provide regional support.  The Trust has enhanced senior leadership though acute assistant director cover over 7 days, with a particular focus on patient flow at Altnagelvin and South West Acute Hospital sites, and has taken steps to cover medical roster gaps through enhanced rates for agency locum cover.My Department has taken a number of steps to improve patient flow at the Altnagelvin Area Hospital, as we recognise the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the ED.An Enhanced Minor Injuries Unit was opened at Altnagelvin Hospital in March 2024 and is a Phone First led service, operating from 8.00am to 9.00pm, 7 days per week.  It is expected that this unit will see 20,000 minor injuries patients per year avoiding the need for these patients to attend the Altnagelvin ED. The Phone First service remains open until midnight every day for patients who are considering attending ED with an urgent but not life-threatening condition.My Department has also commissioned an enhancement to the Altnagelvin Ambulatory Care Unit, operating from 9.00am to 9.00pm, 7 days per week, which is expected to provide almost 12,000 bookable appointments each year. A consultant-led Respiratory Hub is also available at Altnagelvin, with clinics operating two and a half days per week, offering capacity for 1,200 patients each year, as well as providing 7-day respiratory consultant cover for the ED and inpatient referrals.The Western Health and Social Care Trust has established a Control Room in Altnagelvin Hospital to proactively manage the flow of patients through the hospital site and into community services; and a Discharge Co-ordination team is promoting earlier discharges and improved weekend discharge rates for patients who have been assessed as medically fit. In addition, the Trust has engaged with GP practices to embed direct referral pathways from primary care into its Hospital at Home service, enabling patients to receive medical care without the requirement for admission to hospital.  Work between the Trust and care homes is also helping to avoid admissions for residents, with plans in place to manage agreed conditions by care home providers. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any current schemes his Department has to support homeowners over the age of 55.
answer_text: My Department has provided £8 million in Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding to Co-Ownership, our primary delivery partner for intermediate housing, to operate its Co-Own for Over 55s pilot. This novel affordable housing option was designed in response to our ageing population, providing an innovative housing solution for older people, supporting them to live as independently as possible, for as long as possible.Many older people can find themselves living in a home that is no longer suitable for their needs but may not have the equity to buy a new home on their own. With Co-Own for Over 55s, Co-Ownership can help homeowners aged 55 or over to purchase a home that is right for them as they age. This can be an existing or new build property anywhere in Northern Ireland, up to the property value limit of £210,000. The customer will use the equity from the sale of their current property to buy between 50% and 90% of a new home, and Co-Ownership buy the rest, the customer then pays rent on Co-Ownership’s share at below market rates.Further information on the product can be found on Co-Ownership’s website: www.co-ownership.org.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the strategic importance of the Ebrington site in attracting jobs and investment to the North West.
answer_text: The Executive is focused on growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy that is regionally balanced and delivers for every person, every family, in every community, and the North West is a key part of that economic vision. Since taking on the Ebrington site in 2016, TEO has invested over £37 million to transform and regenerate Ebrington.  This investment, along with £40 million of private finance, has been transformative for the site, attracting a variety of new businesses and creating more than 360 jobs.In recent weeks we have seen Alchemy open new offices at Ebrington Plaza, and we were delighted to also attend the opening of Ernst Young offices just last week: a further vote of confidence in our regional talent and infrastructure.The site hosts a vibrant and varied events programme. And in a short time, the new North Atlantic Museum will also open its doors, further strengthening Ebrington’s tourism and cultural offering.The Department will continue to work with tenants and stakeholders to showcase Ebrington, ensuring it supports the region’s rich creativity, innovation, talent and skills, and plays its part in future growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the average waiting times at Emergency Departments between 1 April 2025 and 1 May 2025.
answer_text: My department does not hold data on the average waiting times at emergency departments (ED), but we do monitor our waiting times based on 4-hour and 12-hour performance metrics in EDs. 95% of patients attending any Type 1, 2 or 3 emergency department should be either treated and discharged home, or admitted within four hours of arrival in the department, and no patient attending any emergency department should wait longer than twelve hours. Table 1 shows patient ED attendance and waiting times in each Trust. Regionally for April 2025, 52,207 patients attended EDs and 17,831 patients were seen in less than 4 hours.Table 1Type 1 ED Attendances by waiting time band - April 2526: (01.04.25 to 30.04.25 Inclusive)< 4 Hrs4-12 Hrs> 12 HrsTotal AttendancesBelfast4,5796,2312,38613,196Northern4,1864,6802,12910,995South Eastern1,3883,6102,1227,120Southern4,8575,0012,40312,261Western2,8213,9491,8658,635Regional Total17,83123,47110,90552,207Please note – this data is sourced from SPPG Performance Management Framework for Operational use and includes unvalidated data and is not for official statistics.In 2025/26, I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs. That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. I attended the first of these events on 4 March and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system.The workshop saw attendance from a diverse group of stakeholders within our system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians, and other frontline services. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount being spent on agency social work contracts, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Each of the HSC Trusts has confirmed that they do not spend any of their resources on agency social work contracts. For a number of years, the Department has been working collaboratively with the Trusts and other stakeholders in an effort to stabilize the social work workforce. In 2023 there were over 200 social workers employed by recruitment agencies, delivering HSC services. The majority of these agency social workers were located in Children’s Services, a sector which was also experiencing high levels of vacancies. The Department, in collaboration with the Trusts took the decision to end agency usage for HSC social workers by the end of June 2023. 160 former recruitment agency social workers were then recruited into HSC posts.Since September 2023 to the present day, all of the five Trusts have confirmed to the Department that there have been no recruitment agency social workers employed in HSC services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work her Departmental is undertaking to support and develop reskilling opportunities for BT staff in Derry who face redundancy.
answer_text: I understand that this will be a very difficult and uncertain time for the BT employees. It is important that the impacted employees are aware of the support that is available. My Department’s Careers Service works closely with the Department for the Communities to support anyone facing redundancy or who has been made unemployed. Careers Advisers will be available to provide the employees of BT with personalised professional information, advice and guidance on other local employment, training and education opportunities. This will include exploring the range of upskilling and reskilling opportunities through Assured Skills Academies, Skill Up, Step Up and Breaking Barriers programmes as well as initiates such as job share events in the locality.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what progress has been made to align the dates that offers are made to students applying through the Central Applications Office and UCAS.
answer_text: I welcome the fact that the Leaving Certificate Examination results have moved back to August in recent years, allowing for an earlier Central Applications Office offer process. While there will always be challenges due to the fact that the CAO operates a post-qualification admissions model, and UCAS operates a pre-qualification model of conditional offers, my department will continue to work at both ministerial and official level to address  barriers to cross border mobility. I also welcome the review of A Level equivalencies in the South which means that A-level students are no longer required to achieve four top A-level grades to obtain maximum CAO points.I recently had a very positive meeting with my Ministerial counterpart in the South, James Lawless TD, to discuss North-South Mobility. Both I and Minister Lawless are committed to working together to enhance student mobility in both directions. .Earlier this year, my predecessor Conor Murphy met with Dr Jo Saxton, the Chief Executive of UCAS, who indicated their willingness to work with us on this issue.My Department has already taken action to reduce barriers, including publishing improved guidance for students from here who wish to study in the South. We also introduced a tuition fee loan for postgraduate students from the North studying in the South. Work is ongoing to provide fee support to part-time students from the North studying in the South.My officials continue to work with their colleagues in the south, in UCAS and in the CAO to explore options so that students from Ireland, North and South, can study anywhere on the island.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work his Department is undertaking to repurpose vacant properties to address the housing crisis.
answer_text: I am undertaking a broad programme of work to deliver transformative change across housing, as set out in the Housing Supply Strategy. The Strategy includes a range of enabling actions to deliver the houses we need, including consideration of adaptive reuse of existing buildings for housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25331/22-27, to detail (i) what possible future changes he is referring to; and (ii) a timeline for delivery of these changes.
answer_text: My officials are reviewing the breadth of current funding provided in support of healthcare related courses which lead to a career within HSC. This review is intended to inform options to be presented to me for a public consultation. I would hope to be in a position to launch that consultation in late summer/early autumn.It is not possible to indicate what future changes will be until the consultation has been completed and responses considered.The delivery of future changes is also dependent on the recommendations presented to me. However, any potential changes can only take effect after an appropriate lead-time to facilitate a period of transition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the surplus deficit for all schools in the Foyle constituency for the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my response to AQW 25959/22-27, in which you were provided with the details of the accumulated surplus and deficit position of all schools in the Foyle constituency for the 2023-24 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of vacant properties at present under the management of (i) the Housing Executive; and (ii) other housing agencies.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and Housing Associations. “(i) I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of 29th April 2025, the Housing Executive had 472 actionable and 1357 non-actionable voids. Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated and include properties undergoing change of tenancy repairs and difficult to let properties. Non-actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.(ii) The department does not hold the requested information for Housing Associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be extending the introduction of NHS postage stamps to the North.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25711/22-27, to detail (i) a timeline for the completion of the commissioned review into the Ambulance Service model; and (ii) the scope of this review.
answer_text: I can confirm the following details regarding the commissioned review into the Ambulance Service model, pursuant to AQW 25711/ 22-27:(i) The current clinical capacity review being undertaken within the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is scheduled for completion in June 2025.(ii) The scope of the NIAS Clinical Capacity Review is to optimise NIAS's staffing and resource allocation to efficiently deliver the clinical operating model. This comprehensive review will involve the analysis of historical data, the creation of simulation models, projection of future demand, and benchmarking of NIAS's operations against other services. The primary objective is to identify the most effective balance between different types of ambulance resources to meet patient needs and reduce unnecessary hospital conveyances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding his Department will allocate towards the new cross-border healthcare scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.An initial £10m will be invested in the Scheme for this financial year, allowing people to claim back the costs of overdue treatments arranged outside Northern Ireland, to the value the same procedure would have cost HSC. It is estimated that approximately 1,400 patients could benefit from this investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timeline for reopening a new cross-border healthcare scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the timeline for the active travel route assessment in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My department recently consulted on the Active Travel Delivery Plan which sets out how active travel investment will be prioritised over the next 10 years and beyond. This consultation included plans for the Derry and Strabane District Council area which includes the Foyle constituency. Consultation closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now reviewing the submissions received with a view to submitting a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn.The current programme of pedestrian and cycling measures for the Foyle Constituency is contained within the department’s annual report to Derry and Strabane Council Derry City and Strabane Council Report - 2024 | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many Ambulance Service staff there are per 100,000 people, broken down by health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24696 22-27, for an update on any work his Department is undertaking to address the illegal waste site at Mobuoy.
answer_text: A draft remediation strategy has been developed for the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site, in line with an industry standard process using guidance that applies to Northern Ireland. The next step will be a public consultation.I have approved that officials  proceed to public consultation and the intention is to launch the consultation in Spring. In the meantime, the NIEA continues to implement a comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme (EMP) at the site. This includes site and riverbank inspections, surface water and groundwater monitoring, together with daily laboratory testing of water quality at the NI Water drinking water abstraction point.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24696 22-27, when his Department will publish the draft Optimum Remediation Strategy.
answer_text: I have approved that officials proceed to public consultation on the draft Remediation Strategy and the intention is to launch the consultation in Spring.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24696 22-27, for an update on any engagement his Department has had with residents and businesses impacted by the illegal waste site at Mobuoy.
answer_text: My officials regularly engage with the  Mobuoy Community Stakeholder Group, which includes residents and businesses, to update them on the Environmental Monitoring Programme for the Mobuoy site and on the development of the draft Remediation Strategy.Presentations from engagement meetings are published on the DAERA website and can be found online at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/mobuoy-remediation-project
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to extend bursaries for medical students across the duration of their studies.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to extend bursaries for medical students across the duration of their studies. Any such change would need to be justifiable and affordable in the context of the severe financial pressures currently facing my Department.I have asked my officials to consider possible future changes to the funding of healthcare programmes for which my Department is responsible. While this could potentially impact on the support arrangements for medical students, the scope of any potential change has not yet been determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the collaborative work that can be undertaken with the Minister of Education to work with Gael Linn and Foras na Gaeilge in relation to the development of Irish language in English-medium schools.
answer_text: Foras na Gaeilge have legislative remit in Northern Ireland for the promotion of the Irish language. Any collaborative work undertaken between the Minister of Education, Gael Linn and Foras na Gaeilge in relation to Irish language in English medium schools, would be at the discretion of those parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he is supporting the development of languages in schools as part of the strategic review of the curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum including languages.  I am aware that Ms Crehan has met with key stakeholders  to discuss modern languages as part of her work.   Furthermore, my Department is focused on enhancing language education in Northern Ireland and is currently reviewing proposals developed by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) which aim to strengthen the delivery and uptake of modern languages moving forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with Gael Linn and Foras na Gaeilge in relation to the development of the Irish language in English-medium schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the collaborative work that can be undertaken with the Minister for Communities to work with Gael Linn and Foras na Gaeilge in relation to the development of Irish language in English-medium schools.
answer_text: My Department has not provided any funding to Gael Linn or Foras na Gaeilge in relation to the development of the Irish language in English-medium schools. As education across Northern Ireland continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations, I am not able to commit any funding to Gael Linn or Foras na Gaeilge at this time.Under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, my Department has a duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-medium education. This Article also states “Irish-medium education” means education provided in an Irish speaking school.  There is no such statutory duty placed on my Department to encourage or facilitate the development of Irish language in English-medium schools. In The Belfast Agreement of 1998, it was stated that a North/South Implementation body be set up to promote both the Irish language and the Ulster Scots language. Under the auspices of this body, Foras na Gaeilge will carry out all the designated responsibilities regarding the Irish language. This entails facilitating and encouraging the speaking and writing of Irish in the public and private arena in the Republic of Ireland, and in Northern Ireland where there is appropriate demand, in the context of part three of the European Charter for Regional and Minority Languages.The North/South Language Body is jointly funded by Department for Communities (Northern Ireland) and Department of Tourism, Culture, Arts, Gaeltacht, Sport and Media (Republic of Ireland). Presently, I have no plans to collaborate with the Minister for Communities or his Department on this matter.It is important to note that Irish, as a recognised language of the European Union is classed as one of the languages under the Area of Learning ‘Modern Languages’ which is within Key Stage 3 of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. As with all areas of curriculum content that is taught in schools across Northern Ireland, it is a decision for individual schools to make regarding which particular language(s) under this Area of Learning that they offer and deliver to their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate solutions his Department is putting in place to reduce pressure on the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to improve patient flow at the Altnagelvin Area Hospital, as we recognise the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department.An Enhanced Minor Injuries Unit was opened at Altnagelvin Hospital in March 2024 and is a Phone First led service, operating from 8.00am to 9.00pm, 7 days per week.  It is expected that this unit will see 20,000 Minor Injuries patients per year avoiding the need for these patients to attend the Altnagelvin emergency department.My Department has also commissioned an enhancement to the Altnagelvin Ambulatory Care Unit, operating from 9.00 am to 9.00 pm, seven days per week, which is expected to provide almost 12,000 bookable appointments each year. A consultant-led Respiratory Hub is also available at Altnagelvin, with clinics operating two and a half days per week, offering capacity for 1,200 patients each year, as well as providing 7-day respiratory consultant cover for the emergency department and inpatient referrals.Western Health & Social Care Trust has established a Control Room in Altnagelvin Hospital, to proactively manage the flow of patients through the hospital site and into community services; and a Discharge Coordination team is promoting earlier discharges and improved weekend discharge rates for patients who have been assessed as medically fit. In addition, the Trust has engaged with GP practices to embed direct referral pathways from primary care into its Hospital at Home service, enabling patients to receive medical care without the requirement for admission to hospital.  Work between the Trust and care homes is also helping to avoid admissions for residents, with plans in place to manage agreed conditions by care home providers. As well as re-designating seven general residential beds to increase capacity for patients with dementia, the Trust will open a further 11 beds for dementia patients and eight general nursing beds to help improve flow and discharge from Altnagelvin.All of these measures are aimed at reducing pressure on the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Derry to Dublin air connectivity.
answer_text: In the New Decade New Approach Agreement, the Irish Government committed to taking forward a review of the potential for Government support for air routes from Cork to Belfast and Dublin to Derry.This review was led by the Department of Transport in Dublin in cooperation with the Department for Transport in London which is responsible for Public Service Obligation policy.  Correspondence between my Department and the Department of Transport has confirmed that consideration of the review is still ongoing, and that no decision has as yet been taken. I support the restoration of the Derry to Dublin air route and I welcome the Irish Government’s commitment to its restoration in its Programme for Government earlier this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the upgrade to the Drumahoe to Derry section of the A6.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Part of the works for Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Flagship scheme, between Caw and Drumahoe, will encroach on the illegal waste site at Mobuoy. My Department is currently working with the NIEA and other stakeholders, to ensure that any remediation proposals take account of each parties’ requirements, including those associated with the A6 Phase 2 project.My officials are currently awaiting the publication of DAERA’s Draft Optimum Remediation Strategy.  Once this information is available, my Department will be in a position to consider its implications and develop a delivery strategy and timeline for the A6 Phase 2 project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on all-Ireland student mobility.
answer_text: Question 12 AQO 1821/22-27My department has worked with students to design and publish improved information and guidance for students from the north wishing to apply to higher education in the South. We have also introduced access to a tuition fee loan to postgraduate students from the North studying in the South, and work is ongoing to provide tuition fee support to students from the North studying part time in the South.The Southern Government has been proactive in providing funding for the expansion of Magee and has also improved cross-border mobility by reviewing A-level grade equivalencies. Universities in the South have embraced this and are implementing these changes for applications for the coming academic year.In addition to this progress in Higher Education, Further Education colleges actively build and maintain collaborative cross-border relationships, creating opportunities, pathways and progression routes for student mobility on a cross-border basis.Reducing barriers to all-Ireland student mobility remains a priority for me, and I look forward to meeting with Ministerial counterparts in the South to discuss a joined-up approach, building on the progress already made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to roll-out Mental Health First Aid training in schools.
answer_text: My department has no plans to roll out mental health first aid training in schools.As previously advised, this training programme forms part of the learning and development offer for participating schools under the Education Authority’s ‘Being Well Doing Well’ programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the future sewer improvement works in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water plans to carry out sewer improvements in the Foyle constituency to reline the Culmore Trunk Sewer extending from the Coshowen area to the Queens Quay area which will be undertaken during 2025/26.  This will be carried out using trenchless (underground) techniques from existing manholes.Storm separation projects are also planned in the vicinity of Greenhaw Road/Culmore Road which will commence in early April. Information on these projects and the associated required traffic management will be publicised in advance of works commencing.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the sewer improvements works along the Foyle Road, Derry, will increase capacity for building new homes in the area.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The planned sewer improvements in the Coshowen area will strengthen the network along the Foyle Road in Derry which will help protect against bursts and out-of-sewer flooding.  No additional capacity will be added to the network as part of these planned works.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timeline for the completion of sewer improvements along the Foyle Road and Queen’s Quay area of the Foyle constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether there is sufficient in-patient bed capacity to meet acute care needs at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: Our health and social care services, including inpatient care, are experiencing considerable challenges which will require continued reform in order to both stabilise and better meet the needs of our population.  Ultimately this will require a whole Executive approach as this will undoubtedly require significant investment.  I have recently committed to my Department’s commissioning of a  regional demand and capacity exercise which will include inpatient bed requirements and availability.  However, this exercise has a broader scope as it will include community and acute services in order to better meet the needs of our population. This new exercise builds on the ‘Getting it Right First Time’ review of Emergency Medicine (2024) in partnership with the Department of Health and Public Health Agency, in which all our hospitals with emergency departments were participant.  Whilst this focused on unscheduled services, inpatient beds and patient flow through our hospitals was also considered. Until the demand and capacity exercise is complete I cannot give a categorical answer to the question on bed sufficiency at any one site within the HSC.  This must be viewed as reflected in my Department’s recently closed consultation “Hospital - Creating a Network for Better Outcomes”.  This document makes clear that our health and social care services will deliver more for our population when they are integrated and networked together as centres of excellence,  with capacity considered across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to commission more inpatient beds at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: In answer to the above related questions, my department do not measure numbers of inpatient beds on population figures.  My department commission services and not beds directly.  My Department monitor activity by specialty which relates to activity /occupancy of available beds. Activity /occupancy is monitored by bed capacity as change can happen at certain points due to seasonal requirements, winter pressures or ward closures regarding concerns for infection control.Changes in models of care also has an impact on inpatient beds numbers ie, Hospital at Home service will reduce the number of inpatient beds required as the service sees and treats patients in their own home and prevents hospital admissions therefore reducing the numbers of inpatient beds required.Trusts will routinely increase bed profile on demand by putting in place escalated beds to meet needs.DoH are currently considering undertaking a demand / capacity exercise to assess inpatient beds on a regional basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the metrics his Department and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group use when allocating psychology resources to a Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Better Business Case Northern Ireland guidance is based on the Five Case Model which has been effective from 1st November 2020. The metrics of the Five Case Model are Management – achievable and can be successfully delivered. Strategic – compelling case for change, strategic fit and business needs. Economic – preferred option optimises value for money. Commercial – commercially viable. Financial – affordable within the available funding. A business case is prepared for all new investment decisions before expenditure is committed.  Allocating resources including psychology resources are aligned to the Five Case Model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the sectoral study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland, commissioned by his Department.
answer_text: My predecessor Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre in December 2024 to carry out an independent study into the cost of doing business following the concerns raised in the aftermath of the Chancellors’ Autumn Budget. In particular, employers understandably have significant concerns regarding the impact of the planned changes in Employer National Insurance Contributions.  This is in addition to the other cost pressures they are already facing, such as insurance and energy. The study will provide an independent assessment of which sectors will be hardest hit, and identify any differences compared to Britain.		The research team has been undertaking their analysis and engaging widely, including with business bodies, and the community and voluntary sector, in order to hear the voices of those most impacted as they undertake this research. A final report is expected to be delivered to me by the end of next month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work carried out to assess the viability of the per pupil sixth form funding formula.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics. Post primary pupils that are enrolled in Years 13 or 14 and are following a course leading to an Advanced Level or broadly equivalent to GCE Level 3 qualification, attract a higher Age-Weighted Pupil Unit weighting (2.18) than pupils in Years 8-12 (1.68). This reflects the additional costs associated with educating these pupils, mainly due to the class sizes for post-16 tending to be smaller than for pupils aged 11-16. Education in Northern Ireland faces huge funding pressures. The current education budget is not sufficient to provide a reasonable budget settlement for all schools. In this difficult context, it is my intention to review the Common Funding Scheme, to ensure it distributes the available funding as effectively as possible for the benefit of all pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with further education colleges in relation to the framework Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training.
answer_text: The 14-19 Framework “Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training – A Framework to Transform 14-19 Education and Training Provision” was developed jointly by the Department of Education (DE) and the Department for the Economy (DfE) as a basis on which to drive forward a more strategic approach to 14-19 education and training.  There has been significant engagement with stakeholders right across the 14-19 landscape, including those within the further education sector, both in the development of the Framework and in taking forward the actions contained within it.  Mr Leo Murphy, Principal of North-West Regional College, sits on the Programme Board, which was established to oversee the implementation of the actions contained within the Framework.  Following the Gateway review in early 2024, officials in DE and DfE worked closely with stakeholders to devise an improved delivery model.  This included hosting a workshop for departmental staff and for the wider 14-19 stakeholder group in the Belfast Metropolitan College to devise new governance arrangements to support delivery of work.  Representatives for the Further Education sector attended that workshop.  As a result of that work, new Thematic Working Groups have been established to reflect the key strategic priority areas set out in the Framework Officials from my department and the Further Education sector are represented on the Post-16 Thematic Group.

To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is working to address health inequalities in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: A range of action is being taken by my Department, the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the wider Health and Social Care (HSC) system to address health inequalities in Northern Ireland and the Foyle constituency. This work focuses on people from Inclusion Health groupings, people experiencing socio-economic deprivation, and people experiencing geographical deprivation.Community development approaches are applied flexibly by the PHA within deprived communities, and neighbourhood-based working is utilised to bring together local resources to support joint action. In addition, the provision of the PHA small grant programmes and the delivery of targeted interventions across a range of the key health and well-being issues support locally identified needs.The Derry & Strabane Healthy City & District is part of the World Health Organisation’s European Network of Healthy Cities. This approach recognises the determinants of health and the need to work in collaboration to address the root causes of poor health and health inequalities, working collaboratively to create conditions where everyone can achieve their best possible health.  The approach focuses on action to address the wider determinants of health, which directly impact people’s resilience and wellbeing.In addition to this core work, in July 2024 I announced my intention to develop a ‘Live Better’ approach designed to tackle health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most.This initiative is being delivered in two demonstration areas - one in Belfast (Court DEA) and one in Derry/Londonderry (Moor DEA) - from October 2024 to April 2025.In the Moor DEA this work includes health checks and health fairs planned for January - April 2025, with the first health fair taking place in Sean Dolan's GAC on 28th January 2025. Work is underway to look at interventions to consider children’s oral health, adult prediabetes, and frailty and falls prevention in older people. This work is being developed through partnership working across Primary Care, Western Health & Social Care Trust, the PHA and the Community & Voluntary sector locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department targets funding to adequately support deprived communities.
answer_text: In Health our primary goal is to direct available funding to the areas of greatest need and our priority in recent years has been to minimise the impact on front line services.Funding allocations across existing service level baselines in 2024-25 are directed towards those services where funding is required to meet inescapable commitments and those assessed to be under greatest pressure from unmet demand.The allocation process is that a resource budget is issued to Trusts and other Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) based on their needs rather than to particular subsets of the region. The Trust’s allocations are allocated by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and complemented by a service delivery plan (SDP) setting out the related activities which have been commissioned and are monitored on a monthly basis.Commissioners work with Trusts to ensure that services meet local needs and that the Trusts provide efficient, effective, good quality services as intended by the commissioner within the available funding envelope.The Live Better approach has been designed to help address health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most. This is focusing initially on HSC services and will look at better alignment of, and access to, existing resources and services. Live Better is being delivered within existing resources and contracts.
