question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support his Department provides to oncology services in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) hosts the North West Cancer Centre (NWCC), which provides oncology and haematology services, including radiotherapy and concurrent chemo/radiotherapy, to the WHSCT population. The centre features a joint oncology/haematology inpatient ward with a capacity of 27 beds, a dedicated outpatient unit, and a chemotherapy suite. Additionally, the NWCC offers radiotherapy treatments to both the Western Trust population and a cohort of patients from the Republic of Ireland under an agreed Service Level Agreement.Since 2022 A total of £1.7 million has been allocated to the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) to stabilise their Oncology and Haematology services, funding 29.14 additional whole-time equivalent (WTE) posts across these departments.  This includes Consultant and Specialty Doctor Haematologists, Consultant Radiographers, Pharmacists, Chemotherapy Nurses, Oncology Nurse Practitioner, Haematology Clinical Nurse Specialist (CNS), Acute Oncology Service Nurses, and Acute Oncology Support Workers.Additionally, further investment to support the CNS incremental workforce plan has expanded the cancer CNS workforce within the Western Trust by 5.06 WTE and 4.0 WTE Band 3 support worker posts.This investment will enable increased nurse-led activities, allowing consultants to focus on more complex patient care. It will also facilitate the expansion of the multidisciplinary team (MDT), leading to more streamlined clinical pathways, reduced waiting times, and decreased backlogs. Additionally, the increased staffing of Specialist Doctors will enhance resilience for the on-call rota and reduce reliance on locum shifts.In addition NI Cancer strategy funding has been provided by the my Department to the following roles in WHSCT: An 8a Cancer Service Improvement Lead to support the ongoing implementation of cancer reform and promote a cross-sector approach to the delivery of person-centred care pathways, ensuring co-production and positive patient experience. A Band 7 Cancer Strategy Manager within the cancer planning team. This role will assist in implementing and monitoring the actions determined by the Cancer Programme Board as part of the cancer strategy.Additionally, SPPG of DOH has funded Advanced Communication Skills courses in 23/24 and 24/25, enabling 42 healthcare practitioners to receive this training. This initiative supports Action 53 of the Cancer Strategy (DoH, 2022-2032), which mandates that all professionals engaging in sensitive conversations must undertake advanced communication skills training (ACST). The cancer specialty is one that is frequently and rapidly changing with new medicines (approximately 50 new NICE drugs per annum), emerging technological advancements, innovations in gene therapies makes this a very dynamic clinical space.  Over the period 2018-2023, WHSCT received £554K relating to NICE infrastructure funding for 4.69 wte nursing staff and 5.59 wte pharmacy staff in respec

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps his Department will take to resurface Strand Road, Foyle.
answer_text: My Department has made a significant investment in improving our road network, resurfacing over 300 lane km of roads across the north in the past year. There are currently no plans to resurface the Strand Road.  The last structural survey, undertaken in July 2021, outlined that the residual life and skid resistance of the existing carriageway was 13 years. Further structural survey information is expected in the next few months and my officials will consider this information when it becomes available. In the interim routine inspections of the Strand Road will continue in line with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Meenan Square project.
answer_text: The Meenan Square project will see the construction of a mixed-use development comprising housing, a community hub, and essential local amenities. It is being delivered by the Executive Office’s Urban Villages Initiative, in conjunction with Apex Housing Association and Meenan Square Developments Ltd.A positive public consultation allowing the community to have their say on proposals for the redevelopment of Meenan Square has recently ended. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed Fort George Health Hub.
answer_text: DoH/DoF approval for the Cityside Health and Care Centre (H&CC) project was received in March 2021. This approval gave permission for the purchase of the H&CC site and the appointment of a design team to undertake the initial design RIBA - Stages 2 and 3 concept design and spatial co-ordination. The land for the H&CC building was purchased in March 2023 and all actions relating to Stages 2 and 3 design of the H&CC building are now largely complete, with the exception of car parking and road infrastructure details. This large-scale project is a significant multi-departmental partnership requiring collaborative working to progress the project in the context of the wider development of the Fort George site. The Western Trust continues to work closely with all stakeholders to progress the health and care centre in parallel with master planning activities for the wider Fort George site.The Trust is also working on development of an Outline Business Case 2 to secure approval to progress the design of the project to Stage 4 in 2025, subject to DoH/DfI approval of OBC2 and allocation of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a new Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust is currently refreshing the business case prepared in 2020.  Timescales for implementation will be subject to Departmental approval of this business case and allocation of funding to commence the design process. The current assumption is that a design team will be appointed to lead this process in the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Coshquin Wastewater Treatment Works upgrades, required to connect 2,500 homes in the Cashel development in the Foyle constituency, has a confirmed site and planning permission.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has not secured a site for a new wastewater pumping station at Coshquin and therefore, does not have planning permission to upgrade the wastewater treatment works.The Coshquin Wastewater Treatment Works cannot be upgraded until a site has been identified and secured, and then must go through the planning process. Therefore, the Cashel housing development cannot progress until a site has been identified and planning permission granted.NI Water have been in negotiations with a landowner for its preferred site since August 2023. As these negotiations have been unsuccessful, NI Water is also investigating alternative sites.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a progress update on the continued roll-out of the Minor Injuries Unit at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The new Minor Injuries Unit at Altnagelvin Hospital opened on 25th March 2024. The unit is separate from the existing ED footprint and based in a dedicated area of the Outpatients department, and is led by Emergency Nurse Practitioners and other nursing staff, with oversight from Clinicians from the main Emergency Department, and is operational from 8.00am to 9.00pm each day.  Some of the most common injuries which can be treated in the new Minor Injuries Unit include:•             Minor head injuries.•             Facial injuries including foreign bodies in the eyes or nose.•             Upper and lower limb injuries including fractures, dislocations, bruises and sprains.•             Uncomplicated wounds.•             Bites, burns and scalds.The Minor Injuries unit is a Phone First led service. The Phone First service is designed for patients, including children, who are considering travelling to an Emergency Department or Minor Injury Unit with an injury that requires urgent treatment but is not immediately life threatening.  When you call the Phone First service your condition, or that of the person you are calling on behalf of, will be clinically assessed and arrangements made for you to access the most appropriate service to your needs. This will also include recommending a time to attend, helping minimise waiting times and allowing clinicians to provide the best levels of care for those attending.  The ‘Phone First’ number to ring is 0300 020 6000 while the ‘Phone First’ text relay number is 0870 240 5152. Phone First operates from 8.00am to midnight each day, including weekends and public holidays.The opening of the new unit at Altnagelvin means that there are now separate Minor Injuries streams at all three of the Western Trust’s hospital sites, with Altnagelvin taking its place alongside existing services at South West Acute Hospital and at Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex.From opening up to the end of September 2024, the MIU has seen and treated over 5000 new patients, and an additional 1900 planned reviews.

To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her plans to reform marriage legislation.
answer_text: Members will be aware of my intention to bring forward a Bill to amend the current law on marriage and civil partnership. These important reforms will bring belief marriage within the statutory framework and increase the minimum age for marriage and civil partnership from 16 to 18. I am pleased the Executive approved these proposals at its meeting on 26 September. My officials have finalised instructions to Legislative Counsel and drafting work is now underway. My expectation is that draft legislation on marriage and civil partnership will be introduced to the Assembly during 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans for the next phase of the Communities in Transition programme.
answer_text: Communities in Transition is part of the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime. Since 2019 it has delivered over £26 million of support across eight designated areas, in partnership with community organisations. Delivery to date has been through a thematic approach based on research carried out by academics at that time. The Executive has recently given approval for the overall Programme to be extended for an additional two years until March 2027, subject to funding. Building on the successes to date, we have commissioned a refresh of the evidence base which underpins the delivery of Communities in Transition. In terms of building resilience to paramilitary activity, this includes identifying the most effective interventions to address those issues which are impacting communities the most and looking at where those should be targeted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women’s Health Action Plan, including (i) listening exercises; and (ii) the allocated budget.
answer_text: Due to the severe financial constraints facing my Department, as a direct result of the inadequate 24/25 budget outcome, and the absence of a long term budget, there is currently very limited additional funding available for the Women’s Health Action Plan.  I intend to focus during the remainder of this mandate on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise our Health and Social Care system and address the health inequalities that contribute to poor health outcomes.The Women’s Health Action Plan will therefore focus on the wide range of policy and service development initiatives currently in progress which aim to improve women's health services and outcomes. In terms of the listening exercise that will help inform that work, my officials have been working with Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast to help finalise the survey which will launch later in the Autumn. The Department has provided £10k of funding to support this exercise.Plans are also underway for further stakeholder engagement events on the Action Plan for later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any preventative approaches his Department will adopt to identify heart conditions in women in a timely manner.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to address health inequalities in relation to women’s coronary healthcare.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the first report of the Ulster University Magee Taskforce.
answer_text: The Magee Expansion Taskforce’s interim report launched on 11 September. The report, developed with local people, sets out for the first time what is actually needed to deliver 10,000 students. It estimates that £700m will be needed for new facilities. Around £400m of this is from the public sector but at least £275m could come from the private sector. The Taskforce makes asks of various Departments and Governments. There will now be a dialogue with all involved so that before the end of the year everyone has signed up to a clear and realistic plan to deliver 10,000 students within eight years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their future plans for the Ebrington site.
answer_text: Ebrington has been transformed and future developments, such as a new museum for Derry~Londonderry, will strengthen its economic, social, tourism and cultural impact and value to the city and the North West.An enhanced anchor events programme over the summer has been well attended, and there are several other events planned between now and the Christmas period.  The development of a longer-term anchor programme alongside other large and smaller scale community, charity and commercial events will ensure that Ebrington is well used by local people and visitors alike. We continue to work closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council to consider the ongoing and future management and maintenance of the site.  Discussions are also ongoing with the Council to progress transfer of the Ebrington site.	   A feedback report from recent stakeholder engagements and community workshops in relation to the future of Ebrington is currently being compiled.  This will be shared in the coming weeks and used to inform next steps.     Realising the full potential of Ebrington for the city, the North West and indeed for the region is a shared priority across multiple stakeholders in Derry~Londonderry and the North West.  We are focused on delivering on those shared ambitions and improving processes and partnerships to achieve them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to extend the NHS 111 urgent mental health support number to the north.
answer_text: The Urgent and Emergency Care Review was published in 2022, with one of the strategic priorities being the introduction of local, and ultimately regional, Phone First services, akin to the 111 telephone services provided in other areas of the UK.Local Phone First services are now available in all Trust areas to provide initial advice and triage for those people considering attending urgent care services.  While these services currently operate under different telephone numbers, my Department is also exploring the possibility of introducing a regional HSC111 telephone triage service, which will bring these local services under one, easy to remember telephone number, subject to business case development and funding availability. Officials have been engaging with counterparts in England and Scotland to review how their services currently operate, including the introduction of a mental health hub as part of any new service.  This hub, if introduced, would provide mental health assessment and support, including signposting callers to other relevant mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department will take to support people living with haemochromatosis.
answer_text: Haemochromatosis care in Northern Ireland is provided by a range of specialists in hepatology, gastroenterology and haematology in Trusts as well as care professionals in Primary Care.Patients who are diagnosed with Haemochromatosis require regular monitoring and venesection.  This is delivered by Trusts through ambulatory care services and the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service (NIBTS).  In recent years, treatment pathways have been revised allowing GPs to directly refer suitable patients for venesection at NIBTS rather than attending a consultant led outpatient appointment first.Other support, help and advice is also available via a national patient-led charity, Haemochromatosis UK at: https://www.haemochromatosis.org.uk/helpline

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to promote all-Ireland cooperation for addiction services in the North West.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for the Department of Health, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery’. Under the NI Executive’s Substance Use Strategy, the Department of Health continues to engage with counterparts in Ireland and across the UK to share learning, find common approaches, and to take a collaborative approach, where appropriate, on substance use issues through the British Irish Council and the North South Ministerial Council.Officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and the Public Health Agency have also worked in partnership with stakeholders from across the system to co-produce a new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-2028. This Plan identifies clear strategic priority areas for improving services across the Region, for those impacted by substance use including families and carers.  Implementation of these priority actions will be progressed over the next 4 years subject to funding.Led by the Department of Health, a number of related priority workstreams are currently in progress that will address the needs of people seeking addiction services in the North West and wider border communities and regionally across Northern Ireland. These include;A Population Based Needs Assessment relating to Substance Use in the Western Health & Social Care Trust Area, and;An Independent Review of Tier 4 Substance Use Services which includes In-patient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland.It is the intention that the recommendations from these areas of work along with the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, will support service improvement in the Western Trust area and across the Region, and will help inform and prioritise future funding decisions relating to addictions services on a local and regional level.Department official within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and Public Health Agency (PHA) have further supported a collaborative cross border proposal to the PEACEPLUS Programme for those living with alcohol addiction in our border communities. Under Theme 4 of the European PEACEPLUS Programme: ‘Healthy & Inclusive Communities, Investment Area 4.1 Collaborative Health and Social Care: Addiction Services’, the Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT) partner organisations have submitted a funding proposal to deliver a Community Alcohol Detoxification Project. This proposal, builds on and adds value to existing services and aligns with both the Northern Ireland Executive’s Substance Use Strategy and the HSE’s ‘Reducing Harm, Supporting Recovery‘ Strategy on Substance Use. If successful, it is anticipated that this project will:•	Increase access for those in border communities to community detoxification, including support for abstinence and maintaining recovery. •	Reduce the number

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to increase access to NHS dental appointments.
answer_text: In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

In 2023/24 alone, we invested £7.6 million. These programmes include Urban Villages, Re-development of Ebrington, Communities in Transition, Victims and Survivors, the Central Good Relations Fund, the District Council Good Relations Programme and the Minority Ethnic Development Fund. This investment includes:					Urban Villages, which contributed £1.9 million towards the Gasyard Heritage and Exhibition Centre, which is expected to open later this year; The District Council Good Relations Programme, which provided funding of £269k to deliver programmes across the whole of the council area, including Foyle; The T: BUC Camps programme, which awarded 12 projects a total of £141k; and the Planned Interventions Programme was awarded £127k;The Community Relations and Cultural Diversity Grant scheme awarded £25k to the area; andAlmost £1million has been invested in groups based in Foyle that support victims and survivors.TEO invested £1.5million capital and £1.1million revenue in Ebrington, which has helped to regenerate the site, protect built heritage and attract businesses, jobs, visitors and new experiences to the city. answer_text: Apex Housing Association, funded by the Executive Office, purchased the majority of the site for the Meenan Square Redevelopment project from the private sector landowner Westco Developments in March 2023.The project, delivered by the Executive Office’s Urban Villages Initiative in conjunction with Apex Housing Association and Meenan Square Developments Ltd, will see the construction of a mixed-use development comprising housing, community hub, and essential local amenities. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on an all-Ireland further and higher education provision.
answer_text: Oral Question No 10 – AQO 362/22-27Progress to reduce barriers to North/South student mobility is being made through initiatives in both jurisdictions.For example, from next year postgraduates from the North studying in the South will be able to access a tuition fee loan.The Irish Government is reviewing A-level grade equivalencies – so that students from the north can compete for university places in the South on a more even basis.However, there is more work to do. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the provision for Irish-medium education in Foyle.
answer_text: The statutory responsibility for securing sufficient schools in an area rests with the Education Authority (EA) and, in the case of Irish-medium (IM) education, the Department funds Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) to identify where demand for IM education exists, and to work with parents, and/or in the case of a Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMs), with local schools and the respective educational sector with a view to developing that demand. CnaG was established by the Department in August 2000 and its remit is to encourage and facilitate the strategic development of, and provides guidance and advice to, the Irish medium education sector.Within the Foyle area there are three IM primary schools:-Gaelscoil Éadain Mhóir and Bunscoil Cholmcille (both schools also provide 26 full-time statutory nursery places) and Gaelscoil na Daróige which has a voluntary (non-statutory) Naíscoil providing up to 26 part-time places. In considering Area Planning activity, on 16 April 2024, I approved Development Proposal 685 with a modification that “a statutory nursery unit will be established at Gaelscoil na Daróige to provide 26 part-time nursery places with effect from September 2024 or as soon as possible thereafter”, in effect a change in management type from voluntary provision to statutory provision resulting in no change in provision in the area.For the 2023/24 year the three schools have a combined approved enrolment of 447 places with an actual combined enrolment of 398 pupils and there are 57 available places.In 2022/23 the schools admitted 54 pupils into Year One for the 66 approved admission places.At the conclusion of the stage one process for Primary Admission to Year One, the three schools have received 66 First Preference applications for the 66 approved places for the 2024/25 year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department will facilitate access to cross-border addiction services in the North West.
answer_text: I provided a wider overview of the work underway in relation to cross -border addiction services in the North West in AQW 6416/22-27, and can provide the following detail by way of a further update.The Co-operation and Working Together (CAWT) proposal to the EU Peace Plus Programme for a funded Cross Border Community Detoxification project is in an advanced stage of development by all partners and is due to be submitted to the Special European Programme Board (SEUPB) for approval later this month. It is anticipated that if successful, this project will:Increase access for those in border communities to community detoxification, including holistic support for abstinence and maintaining recovery. Reduce the number of unplanned and unsupervised detoxifications in both community and hospital settings. Reduce the length of stay and bed occupancy in acute inpatient hospital bed following commencement of medical alcohol detoxification. Provision of rapid community follow up to optimise community engagement, reduce DNA rate, and promote early and sustained intervention.An Independent Review of Regional Tier 4a and Tier 4b, Addiction Services in Northern Ireland is close to completion. The purpose of the Review is to provide direction on a best practice model for in-patient detoxification and residential rehabilitation addiction services in the Region which addresses the current needs of our population. This Review includes the provision of residential rehabilitation services as provided by Northlands in Derry/Londonderry and hospital in-patient detoxification services as provided by the Asha Unit within Tyrone and Fermanagh Hospital in Omagh.In addition, a Needs Assessment for the Population of the Western Health and Social Care Trust area specific to substance use has been completed. The recommendations from this Assessment will be taken forward in 2024 to address the needs of those who are affected by problematic substance use, whether their own or someone else’s, across the Western Health and Social Care Trust area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of salaries of medical technicians in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, compared to those in other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust is committed to ensuring that the NHS Job Evaluation Scheme introduced under Agenda for Change is applied, ensuring pay structures remain consistent and non-discriminatory.The Job Evaluation of Posts within Trusts are determined in line with the principles within the NHS Job Evaluation Handbook. This involves information relating to a post being presented to a panel of trained matchers to determine the band for the role.  Panels adopt a joint partnership approach with management and Trade Union side representation, under the principles outlined under a ‘HSCNI regional Job Matching & Job Evaluation Protocol’.
