Whilst posts may share job titles across Trusts, the requirements of these posts in different Trusts may not be the same.Across Trusts, Medical Technicians are employed between bands 4-7 and the salary ranges are listed below;Job TitleEntry Salary Point*Top Salary Point*MEDICAL TECHNICAL OFFICER (BAND 4)£23,949£26,282MEDICAL TECHNICAL OFFICER (BAND 5)£27,055£32,934MEDICAL TECHNICAL OFFICER (BAND 6)£33,706£40,588MEDICAL TECHNICAL OFFICER (BAND 7)£41,659£47,672*Figures as at 25/03/2024 – before a proposed pay increase for 2023/24.Medical Technicians are employed in Trusts across a range of specialities including Anaesthetics/ICU/Theatres, Audiology, Cardiology, Cardiac Surgery, Central Sterilisation Services Department, Dental, Electrocardiogram, Genetics, Hospital Mortuary & Cardiac Physiology, Hospital Sterilisation and Decontamination Unit, Lab Services, Medical Physics, Nephrology, Paediatrics, Pharmacy, Physiology and Vascular Surgery, Pulmonary Function etc.The roles and responsibilities will vary according to the area of specialism and the level of post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of students that are enrolled in the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) for the current academic year; and (ii) the number of enrolments from students from the Republic of Ireland, broken down by CAFRE campus.
answer_text: In the academic year 2023/2024 CAFRE currently has 1766 students enrolled on education programmes.  There are 80 students from the Republic of Ireland with  57 studying at the Enniskillen Campus, 21 at the Greenmount Campus, Antrim and 2 at the Loughry Campus, Cookstown.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to bring forward legislation to amend the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022.
answer_text: I have no intention to bring forward legislation to amend the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to amend regulations relating to student accommodation.
answer_text: My Department does not regulate student accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the tuition fee charged to College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise students for (a) part-time; and (b) full-time study; and (ii) the current level of financial support available for (a) part-time; and (b) full-time students.
answer_text: CAFRE Further Education (FE) part and full-time courses do not attract a tuition fee. Means tested FE financial support information is available at: https://www.eani.org.uk/fegrants.  CAFRE Higher Education (HE) Course fees for full and part time students, and financial support available for eligible students, are detailed on the CAFRE website: www.cafre.ac.uk/student-life/funding-support/.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the capital projects in the North West that will be advanced using capital funding allocations.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward the following capital projects, which have business case approvals and funding to proceed:The Inner Walled City Public Realm project will deliver £5 million improvements to the streetscape inside the Historic City Walls in Londonderry.The £1 million Harbour Square Environmental Improvement scheme will improve the public realm in front of the Guildhall in Londonderry and deliver an art piece. My Department is also contributing £500,000 towards the Museum at Ebrington in Londonderry.My Department’s Capital Contributions will also support Social Housing Development Programme projects in the North-West.In addition, the member will be aware of the NI Executive commitment to City Deal and Inclusive Future Fund investment for Londonderry and Strabane.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department is progressing funding for cross-border addiction services in the North West.
answer_text: Under the NI Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery’, my Department engages with counterparts in Ireland and across the UK to share learning, find common approaches, and take joined up action, where appropriate on these issues through the British Irish Council and the North South Ministerial Council.Led by my Department, a number of priority strategic workstreams are currently in progress that will address the needs of people seeking addiction services in the North West, the border communities and across Northern Ireland. Under theme 4 of the European PEACEPLUS Programme: ‘Healthy & Inclusive Communities, Investment Area 4.1 Collaborative Health and Social Care: Addiction Services’, Cooperation and Working Together (CAWT); partner organisations are currently working collaboratively to develop a cross border proposal to deliver a Community Detox Project for those living in our border communities. This proposal,  which builds on and adds value to existing services is in line with the NI Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery’ and it is anticipated that if successful, this project will:Increase access for those in border communities to community detoxification, including holistic support for abstinence and maintaining recovery. Reduce the number of unplanned and unsupervised detoxifications in both community and hospital settings in border communities. Reduce the length of stay and bed occupancy in acute in-patient hospital beds,  following commencement of medical alcohol detoxification. Provide rapid community follow up to optimise community engagement, increase attendance at services, and promote early and sustained intervention.In addition to the CAWT proposal, my Department will seek to deliver the relevant priority actions from the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan, and the forthcoming Substance Use Needs Assessment for the Population of the Western Trust Area, and the Review of Tier 4 Addiction Services in Northern Ireland, with the aim of providing a co-ordinated approach to improving pathways of care and models of support for people who require addiction services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on plans to develop a new build for Ballymagroarty Hazelbank Community Partnership, Derry; and (ii) what efforts his Department will take to ensure the completion date is met.
answer_text: I can advise that Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading on the development of the Ballymagroarty Hazelbank Community Centre.  I understand that there have been some discussions with my officials but at this time the project does not have an up-to-date business case and there is a significant funding shortfall.I can confirm that my officials will continue to support the Council as they progress the project and will attend project meetings along with other relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what strategies his Department will put in place to ensure emergency dental treatment can be accessed by all patients.
answer_text: At present, all registered patients can access emergency dental treatment at their registered practice, where their General Dental Practitioner has an obligation to provide the required treatment to in a timely manner.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice. For patients experiencing an urgent or emergency dental condition at weekends or public holidays, additional support can be obtained at one of the three Emergency Dental Clinics (EDCs), where patients will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given, or treatment arranged.In addition, undergraduate dental students, based in the School of Dentistry in Belfast, provide emergency dental appointments for unregistered patients under the supervision of dental teaching staff. Patients can self-refer to this service, which is available during the university term time. A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services – which should also have the effect of increasing access – and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many adults are awaiting an ADHD diagnosis.
answer_text: There are currently no regionally commissioned services for adult ADHD and therefore no requirement for HSC Trusts to manage a dedicated and comparable ADHD waiting list. The information is therefore not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients are on a waiting list for a NHS dentist, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The information requested is not available.  Most dental care is delivered through general dental practitioners, who are independent contractors and there is therefore no way of collecting information on waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what strategies his Department will put in place to recruit and retain NHS dentists.
answer_text: My Department published the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People in May 2018.  The aim of the Strategy is that ‘by 2026, we meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.The Strategy’s second action plan, covering the period 2022/23 to 2024/25, was published on 15th June 2022 following detailed engagement with stakeholders from across the health and social care system.The second action plan contains a range of actions that address recruitment and retention of staff across health and social care.  These include actions to develop focussed campaigns to showcase and support recruitment into a career in health and social care including dentistry and to develop retention initiatives for specific professions including our dental workforce.A Consultation was opened on 16 February 2024 by UK government to give the General Dental Council the necessary statutory powers to set up a new status of provisional registrant for individuals holding an overseas dentistry qualification and who have not yet satisfied the GDC’s requirements for full registration, through the creation of a separate list in the dentists register. As well as bringing in valuable additional workforce, provisional registration would also help overseas-qualified dentists to adapt to practising in the UK environment with support and supervision. Individuals would be registered with GDC with ‘provisional registration’ status.Regarding retention, the absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is supporting adults awaiting an ADHD diagnosis.
answer_text: Although there are currently no commissioned services for Adult ADHD, provision has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. While this has ensured a level of provision is available, the Department is aware that this has also led to inconsistencies between Trusts including pathways and gaps between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD, leading to individuals waiting longer than intended for assessment and support.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.SPPG alongside Policy colleagues in the Department are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what strategies (i) are in place; and (ii) will be put in place to support people under the age of 65 who are diagnosed with dementia.
answer_text: 1 in 3 people will develop dementia in their lifetime and by 2040 the number of people living with dementia in Northern Ireland is expected to double from 22,700 to 43,000.  While dementia is often seen as a disease of old age, about 5% of people diagnosed with dementia are under 65, often referred to as early onset dementia. It is recognised that early onset dementia poses additional and specific challenges as these individuals are often in employment and may have  family and other financial commitments and therefore have additional and specific support needs. In terms of the current strategies in place to support people under the age of 65 who are diagnosed with dementia, each individual’s support needs will be person- centred in both the pre-diagnosis  and post diagnosis stages of the condition. All individuals have a HSC Trust key worker who will agree the care plan and the range of supports in partnership with the individual. The key worker will ensure that the individual has access to any additional supports provided by other statutory bodies and the community and voluntary sector. The range of supports includes practical, financial and emotional supports and each individual will be supported by  a dementia navigator who will signpost or refer to the appropriate services and supports. In terms of what strategies will be put in place to support people under the age of 65 who are diagnosed with dementia, my officials are progressing a Regional Dementia Project Board which will consider future service delivery requirements including any gaps in the current range of supports to those with early onset dementia.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department will do to reduce waiting lists for dementia diagnosis.
answer_text: The Department acknowledges that dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system. Northern Ireland is projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040, with the current figures of 22,700 set to rise to 43,000. This is due to the fact that the size of the older population in Northern Ireland is expected to increase much faster than the rest of the UK. The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen in order to understand their condition and to commence treatment.  The Department has issued a 9 week- target commissioning plan directive which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within that timescale and SPPG continues to hold HSC Trusts accountable for their performance against this target.  At the end of January 2024 there were 2,299 people awaiting a first assessment with 1,421 of these waiting greater than 9 weeks.   The breaches are largely confined to two of the HSC Trusts and SPPG have agreed improvement plans with both Trusts in efforts to improve the level of performance. However the significant workforce pressures in these Trusts within a number of the professional groups most crucial to delivering diagnosis is contributing to the delays in assessment and diagnosis. SPPG are involved in ongoing discussions with the Royal College of Psychiatry in regard to the shortfall in the Psychiatry workforce.My Officials continue to engage with Trusts’ Dementia Leads and plan to  take forward a Regional Dementia Project Board to consider potential future service delivery models which will include considerations of the assessment and diagnostic elements of the dementia pathway. The Regional Dementia Care Pathway published in 2018 offers a transformational service delivery model incorporating a primary care led approach working closely with secondary care dementia services to deliver assessment, diagnosis, treatment and management of dementia. It is acknowledged that implementation of the pathway should support more timely assessment and diagnosis of dementia but is reliant on significant financial investment. In the interim SPPG continues to work with HSC Trusts to consider where efficiencies can be made across the current dementia care pathway to increase capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department will do to support those on the waiting list for a dementia diagnosis.
answer_text: An early and timely diagnosis for dementia is essential to ensure people with the illness can access the right information, treatment and support. The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen and acknowledges the need to support those people on memory waiting lists across the region.The Department has issued a 9 week- target commissioning plan directive which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within that timescale.  The Department is aware that at the end of January 2024 there were 2,299 people awaiting a first assessment with 1,421 of these waiting greater than 9 weeks.  All Trusts operate a triage system to ensure that those individuals assessed as highest risk are given priority in terms of further assessment and management. In order to support those on waiting lists, HSC Trusts work closely with other statutory bodies such as the GP Federation multi-disciplinary Teams as well as community and voluntary sector organisations such as Dementia NI, Age NI and NI Alzheimer’s Society. The range of supports includes practical and emotional supports through peer support services, carer support groups, information, guidance and awareness sessions. In addition a range of publications are available on the NI Direct Dementia webpage  including; What to do if you're worried someone has dementia and 10 common signs of dementia, and the statutory and community and voluntary organisations will signpost to these information resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of sites in the Foyle constituency which are being treated for Japanese knotweed.
answer_text: Landowners are responsible for managing Japanese knotweed on their own land and under current Northern Ireland legislation, my Department does not have any legal imperative to make them do so. As it is not actually an offence to have Japanese knotweed present on your land, landowners are not obliged to share that information with my officials, unless it is being managed as part of an active planning application. Therefore a breakdown of sites in the Foyle constituency which are being treated for Japanese knotweed would not be available to this Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Mobuoy waste site.
answer_text: My department is continuing to progress work in relation to monitoring the site, pursuing a criminal justice outcome against the former operators, and planning for remediation.Safeguarding public health, protecting the water quality of the river Faughan and working in partnership with NI Water to safeguard drinking water in the north-west are of utmost importance.I intend to issue a draft Optimum Remediation Strategy, developed by my department’s appointed Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT), for public consultation within the next 2 months. This strategy, to deliver long term remediation of the site, is based on the best balance of environmental, social, and economic factors.The public consultation will give all interested parties the opportunity to comment on the draft Optimum Remediation Strategy. There is no agreed preferred option, and there will not be, until consultation has been completed. Responses will be carefully considered and presented for the next stages of scrutiny and approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what schemes his Department will put in place to incentivise home owners to install renewable energy sources.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity as outlined in the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022 and 2023. My officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects. Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.My department is also preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023.  The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.Finally, homeowners may wish to seek independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as the NI Energy Advice Service, the Energy Saving Trust and Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on capital projects in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: There are six schools in the Foyle constituency that have been announced under the Major Capital Works or School Enhancement programmes. The current status of these projects are as follows:Major Capital Works Programme Ardnashee School and College new build project has commenced onsite and is expected to complete in Spring 2025.Lumen Christi College and St Brigid's College, Londonderry are two of twenty-eight schools announced to advance in planning in March 2022.  All twenty-eight projects were subsequently paused in April 2023, due to the capital budget position.  On 12 February 2024 I advised that seven of the projects from the March 2022 announcement would now progress in planning, however Lumen Christi and St Brigid’s College were not one of the seven projects and thus these projects remain paused.School Enhancement Programme (SEP)Chapel Road Primary School was announced in May 2018. The proposed project involves a new extension and some refurbishment of the existing school buildings to address priority issues.  Planning approval has been received and the scheme is currently at the technical design and pre-tender state (RIBA Stage 4).Greenhaw Primary School was also announced in May 2018. The proposed project involves refurbishment and reconfiguration of existing areas, together with an extension.  The scheme is currently at spatial co-ordination design stage (RIBA Stage 3).Holy Child Primary School was announced in January 2019.  The scoping report has recently been agreed with the school and the procurement of a design team for the project is progressing. Without additional capital funding those projects that are in the design stages will not progress to construction and the twenty-one remaining projects from the March 2022 announcement will remain paused.  Should capital funding become available projects will be reviewed and prioritised for delivery.  As I have referenced on a number of occasions, I will be bidding for additional capital funding to deliver these much needed projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to increase statutory support for women experiencing menopause.
answer_text: As I outlined during an Assembly debate on 13 February about the development of a Women’s Health Strategy, I recognise the impact that menopause can have on women. There are many examples of good practice in terms of menopause care within our system, but it is not consistent, and that means that it is not equitable. My officials have been engaging with primary and secondary care clinicians to benchmark what is available in each Trust and to consider how to standardise best practice care across the region.Whilst the majority of women will be able to manage their symptoms with the help of their GP, there is a cohort of women who will need specialist hospital care and, for those women, waiting lists are unacceptable.To help address that issue, I want to prioritise implementation of the recently published Gitting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology report. My Department has already written to all Trusts about the report and will engage with relevant stakeholders to progress implementation as far as possible within the available resources. Menopause care will also form part of the Women’s Health Action Plan that my Department is developing that builds on ongoing work and identifies the priority actions across women’s health services that can be taken forward within the current budget, as well as those actions for which additional investment will be required.  A copy of the Hansard report on the debate can be found at Official Reports (niassembly.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress at the proposed Fort George health hub.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the Fort George Health Hub was approved by the Department of Health on 15 March 2021.  The Western Health & Social Care Trust (WHSCT) appointed an Integrated Consultancy Team to design the new Hub on 24 November 2021. WHSCT have been working with the Department for Communities as the design of the new Health Hub has progressed to review the impact of the inclusion of the Health Hub on the wider masterplan for the Fort George site.A submission for a “Pre-Application Discussion” (PAD) with the Planning Authority regarding the application for the Health Hub and its impact on the wider site masterplan was lodged by DfC on 28 September 2022.   WHSCT purchased the portion of the site required to build the Health Hub from DfC in March 2023. The design of the Health Hub has progressed, and is ready to be submitted for Planning Approval.  Discussions with statutory authorities through the PAD process regarding the wider masterplan and the Health Hub remain ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what his Department will do to support mental health and wellbeing in schools.
answer_text: The mental health and wellbeing of all our pupils is a high priority for me.The joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ was published in February 2021 and provides overarching guidance to support those working in educational settings to help them promote emotional wellbeing and strengthen self-esteem and resilience in our children and young people.There were a number of projects and services established under the Framework to help schools support the emotional health and wellbeing of pupils, including the recently commenced Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools and the Being Well Doing Well programme which focuses on a whole-school approach.  Additionally, the Independent Counselling Service for Schools is available to post-primary age pupils.  My Department will continue to invest in the joint Framework to further embed emotional health and wellbeing in education and to work with the Department of Health on their Mental Health Strategy for the whole population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will explore enhanced north-south cooperation to address people living in Derry who are unable to access free addiction treatment in Donegal where capacity is available.
answer_text: A number of strategic workstreams are in progress that will address the needs of people seeking addiction services in the North West and rest of Northern Ireland. Under PEACEPLUS Theme 4: Healthy & Inclusive Communities, Investment Area 4.1 Collaborative Health and Social Care: Addiction Services, CAWT partner organisations HSE, PHA, SPPG, SHSCT and WHSCT are currently working collaboratively to develop a cross border proposal to deliver a Community Detox Project for those living in our border communities, which builds on / adds value to existing services. The proposal is in line with the NI Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery’ and it is anticipated that if successful, this project will:•	Increase access for those in border communities to community detoxification, including holistic support for abstinence and maintaining recovery. •	Reduce the number of unplanned and unsupervised detoxifications in both community and hospital settings. •	Reduce the length of stay and bed occupancy in acute inpatient hospital bed following commencement of medical alcohol detoxification. •	Provision of rapid community follow up to optimise community engagement, reduce DNA rate, and promote early and sustained intervention.In addition to the CAWT proposal, relevant priority actions from the HSC Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan, the Western Trust Area Substance Use Needs Assessment, and the Review of Tier 4 Addiction Services in Northern Ireland will be taken forward in 2024 with the aim of providing a co-ordinated approach to improving pathways of care and models of support for people who require addiction services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures he will put in place to increase the number of enrolments on Allied Health courses at Magee.
answer_text: My Department’s 'Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People', commits to an ongoing programme of strategic workforce planning through which future workforce needs are identified. This process also provides an evidence base upon which proposals for investment in pre-registration education and training programmes, such as the Allied Health Professions, can be developed subject to the necessary funding being made available.I will continue to make the case for investment in our future workforce, including the Allied Health Professions. However, as the 2024/25 budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set, I am unable, at present, to make any commitment to expand commissioned places.  I anticipate that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he will have with further and higher education institutions to resource their mental health and wellbeing supports for students and staff.
To ask the Minister of Education how his Department will support Irish-medium education in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: A range of bespoke, sector specific investment is provided to support the development of Irish-medium education.  This includes annual funding support to Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) which also represents the sector on Area Planning groups, specific support to Irish-medium Units, funding to the Education Authority and CCEA, and early years funding to Altram which has developed a range of pre-school resources aimed at helping immersion learning.My Department seeks to respond positively to parental demand for Irish-medium provision and works to meet the needs of the sector, for example in considering home to school transport requirements and school requests for temporary variations to their approved numbers.  Specifically in relation to the Foyle constituency, minor works projects at Gaelscoil na Daróige and Bunscoil Cholmcille, representing a capital investment of circa £3.4m, are currently going through the business case approvals process; subject to approval, delivery will however be dependent on the availability of budget.  A site search has been commissioned for a potential new location for Gaelscoil Éadain Mhóir.  My Department is currently considering an Irish-medium Development Proposal to establish a statutory nursery unit at Gaelscoil na Daróige to provide 26 part-time nursery places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Health Workforce Strategy will include interventions to increase the number of enrolments on Allied Health courses at Magee.
answer_text: My Department’s 'Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People', commits to an ongoing programme of strategic workforce planning through which future workforce needs are identified. This process also provides an evidence base upon which proposals for investment in pre-registration education and training programmes, such as the Allied Health Professions, can be developed subject to the necessary funding being made available.I will continue to make the case for investment in our future workforce, including the Allied Health Professions. However, as the 2024/25 budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set, I am unable, at present, to make any commitment to expand commissioned places.  I anticipate that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress of the Substance Use Strategy arising from the 2021 report Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery.
answer_text: I launched Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery in September 2021 as Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy. As part of the delivery of the strategy’s outcomes, I tasked the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group to co-produce a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework, covering all tiers of service provision. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, which is currently being finalised after a public consultation, will:•	ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;•	ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;•	ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines;•	provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; and•	recognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.To date an additional £750,000 has been invested recurrently in the Strategy’s implementation. Two other priority pieces of work are also due to report shortly – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland. The recommendations from both reports will help inform the final Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new commissioning framework. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services provision, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) provision is co-ordinated within an integrated five Stepped Care Pathway. The pathway is for everyone involved with and receiving emotional and well-being services across Northern Ireland. The pathway provides assessment, treatment and consultation to children, young people, families and the wider professional network.NORTHERN TRUSTEmotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools (EWTS) pilot is part of a continuum of services across health and education which have been developed to support the implementation of the Framework for Children & Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education. Each service within the framework has been developed to complement the others and to ensure the needs of children and young people, their families and school staff are met.  EWTS are dedicated to supporting a number of identified post primary schools at any one time, for a specific timeframe, through promoting the positive psychological, emotional and mental health of children. This will include building individual and collective resilience and supporting capacity within educational staff by equipping staff with the knowledge, skills and ability to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity for children and young people with emerging emotional and mental health concerns.  The overall aim is for a reduction in the growth of longer term mental health difficulties for children and young peopleChildren’s Early Intervention Service (CEIS)CEIS provides Step 2 support and advice to children and families who are experiencing emotional and/or behavioural difficulties which are impacting on the child, young person or family functioning.  It is a community based, multi-disciplinary service, providing structured self-help approaches, behavioural therapy, and/or family support, to reduce the impact of problems and prevent their escalation to greater/more significant difficulties. The service also provide group based interventions for children, young people and parents in line with best practice in early intervention.Step 3 Core CAMHSCore CAMHS works with children and young people at Step 3 who are experiencing moderate to severe mental health difficulties which are impacting on their day to day life.  The overall aim is to provide support and intervention for emotional and mental health needs by working collaboratively with the young person, their families and carers.  This is a community based, multi-disciplinary service providing specialist, evidence based treatment in response to a range of mental illnesses. The Crisis Resolution Team (CRISIS)CRISIS will offer a rapid assessment to emergency mental health referrals, and if appropriate, will follow up with an intensive, proactive, time limited approach to engagement with young people and their families who would benefit from treatment in the community.  This includes risk assessment and management, as well as offering a com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide additional funding for addiction services in Derry.
answer_text: Under a commitment for my Department within New Decade, New Approach, I launched Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery in September 2021.To address a key action within the strategy, the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group have produced, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan, to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan will: •	ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;•	ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;•	ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; •	provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; and•	recognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.In addition, two early pieces of priority work are being finalised – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland. These will inform the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new commissioning framework. These forthcoming pieces of work will also be used to inform proposals for the potential commitment from the UK Government in Annex A of New Decade, New Approach. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being available into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the plans to develop a new build for the Glen Community Centre, Derry; and (ii) what efforts his Department will take to ensure the completion date is met.
answer_text: Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading on the development of the Glen Community Centre but I understand that while the application for planning permission for the facility has been made it has not been approved at this time.I can confirm that my officials will continue to support the Council as they progress the project and will attend project meetings along with other relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; (ii) whether the Ryan McBride Brandywell Stadium will be included in the funding; and (iii) a timeline on the next steps for the programme.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society. I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this commitment has not yet been delivered. Work to finalise proposals, including eligibility is on-going. However, I expect that all clubs whose grounds are located in Northern Ireland will be within scope.I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to reinstate the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department has taken to address the backlog in teacher registrations.
answer_text: The Department is not aware of any current issues with teacher registration backlogs. There was a period of more widespread delays in mid-2021 due to essential upgrades to GTCNI’s IT systems but these were quickly resolved, and no large-scale registration delays have occurred since then. Incomplete information or delays in third party validations can slow the processing of individual registrations, however, all applications are now closely monitored to highlight any unusual delays and allow prompt remedial action to be taken.In April 2023 GTCNI introduced a new registration system, which includes an online application portal, further enhancing its registration process and improving communications with applicants at all stages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department has taken to support substitute teachers during the ongoing payment processing issue with the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register.
answer_text: This question relates to events in August 2022.A new and improved Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) was procured by the Education Authority (EA) which administers the system, and it went live in August 2022.A number of issues were identified when the system went live which resulted in the late salary payments for some supply teachers. These issues were soon resolved. NISTR is accepted as the only system through which schools can engage supply teachers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration her Department has given to an interim payment for substitute teachers who have not been paid since August 2022.
answer_text: The Department funds the Education Authority to administer the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) which is effectively a booking system enabling schools to source and engage professionally qualified teachers who have undergone a range of pre-employment checks.  On the third working day of each month, a file of approved (signed-off) bookings is extracted from the NISTR and sent to the Department for processing of salary to temporary teachers on schools’ behalf.There have been a number of issues regarding the implementation of a new NISTR system and the timely payment of temporary teachers. The Department and the EA continue to work together intensively, alongside stakeholder groups, to ensure that immediate steps are taken to ensure staff are paid for their valuable service.  In the current exceptional circumstances, particularly in light of the cost-of-living crisis, the Department has asked the EA to process interim emergency payments to all teachers impacted as a matter of urgency.Due to administrative errors in extracting the file and uploading it to the payroll system; 309 temporary and supply teachers received only partial salary on 18 October for bookings that were approved by schools and should have been paid under normal circumstances.  The EA has sincerely apologised for this error and is processing interim emergency payments to reach these teachers by 25 October.The other factor that has caused problems in the timely payment of temporary teachers relates to the fact that the appropriate approval processes at school level were not completed in line with the payroll deadline.  This prevented payment being made on the planned date for an estimated 300 teachers.  Incorrect data input in respect of hours worked also appears to have featured in the sign-off process.  The EA is contacting all relevant schools to seek confirmation of attendance, check period of service and obtain other necessary sign-off information to enable further interim emergency payments to pursued.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many do not resuscitate orders were applied during: (i) 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2019; (ii) 1 April 2019 to 31 March 2020; (iii) 1 April 2020 to 31 March 2021; and (iv) 1 April 2021 to 31 March 2022.
answer_text: Information on Do Not Attempt Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation decisions is recorded on individual patients’ notes.  This information is not collated by Health and Social Care Trusts and could only be obtained through a manual review of patients’ records.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to outline what accessibility measures are available within Personal Independence Payment assessments; and (ii) whether these measures include an easy read format.
answer_text: Information can be requested in an alternative format such as large font, audio CD or Braille from both my Department and Capita, the PIP assessment provider.  In addition, both organisations also have the Video Relay Service that provides access to British and Irish Sign Language interpreters when making a claim, enquiring about PIP or when undergoing an assessment with Capita.A series of videos are also available to help explain the key stages when claiming PIP. These videos are available in British and Irish Sign Language and with subtitles. These are available through the nidirect website at www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/personal-independence-payment-pip#toc-16. My Department and Capita have also published Easy Read information leaflets and guidance about PIP. These easy read guides explain how you can get help from PIP and how to claim it. These can be found by accessing the NIDirect website at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/pip-easy-read-guides and on Capita’s PIP website at https://www.capita-pip.co.uk/en Customers can also avail of the services offered by the ‘Make the Call’ team, who can assist with making a claim and filling out forms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what recruitment campaigns have been run to attract dementia consultants to work in the north.
answer_text: Recruitment is a matter for HSC Trusts as employers. The care and treatment of patients with dementia would fall mainly within the role of psychiatrists who specialise in the care of older people. Geriatricians and Consultants in Elderly Care who have a specialist interest in the diagnosis and management of Dementia may also have a role depending on individual Trust structure.  The South Eastern Trust, the Belfast Trust and the Northern Trust have had no recruitment drives in this financial year.The Western Trust has recently advertised unsuccessfully for 2 Consultants in Old Age Psychiatry which closed on 9 September 2022. These posts will now be re-advertised.The Southern Trust also undertook a recruitment campaign for Consultants in Psychiatry of Old Age in May 2022 – this was also unsuccessful.  The Clinical Director, Psychiatry of Old Age Services, along with others, hosted a stand at the Royal College of Psychiatrists International Congress in Edinburgh in June 2022, with the aim to encourage psychiatrists outside of Northern Ireland, to consider job opportunities available in the Southern HSC Trust.  The Trust will be commencing another recruitment campaign for Consultants in Psychiatry of Old Age in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the waiting times for a dementia diagnosis across all Trust areas.
answer_text: The number of active waits for a dementia diagnosis appointment, as at end of August 2022 (the latest month held) is set out in Table 1.Table 1: Number of active waits for a dementia diagnosis appointment, as at 31st August 2022HSC Trust0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks>9weeksTotalBelfast1109797184343Northern917784208460South Eastern7086638601,079Southern634740114Western604539666810Source: PMSID (SPPG) Monthly Mental Health Outpatient Waiting Times. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the (i) dates; and (ii) findings of reviews into COVID-19 restrictions in courtrooms.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) reviewed and updated its COVID-19 mitigations in courtrooms on an ongoing basis throughout the pandemic as regulations were amended and to ensure that its approach remained consistent with public health advice.Following changes to the Executive’s guidance on working from home in June 2022, in July 2022 the NI Civil Service (NICS) introduced new ‘NICS Workplace Advice: Live Life COVID-Aware’ guidance.As a result, NICTS reviewed and updated its COVID guidance and in August 2022 introduced new ‘Autumn/Winter 2022 Courts and Tribunals Venue Safety’ guidance. This is available to all court and tribunal users at:https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/keeping-nicts-buildings-safe-secure-and-clean-during-covid-19No further changes to NICS guidance has been recommended since that time.  I will continue to monitor the situation in line with advice and guidance from the Public Health Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide the rationale for the continuation of the one metre social distancing rule within courtrooms.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) continues to deploy a range of measures across its venues to mitigate against transmission of coronavirus. These measures are based on public health advice, and the NI Civil Service (NICS) ‘NICS Workplace Advice: Live Life COVID-Aware’ guidance.This guidance is aimed at ensuring a consistent approach across the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) and a common interpretation of current health advice within the workplace environment.In relation to actions to help reduce the virus spreading in the workplace, the guidance notes that mitigation measures may include some form of social distancing, ideally of no less than 1m where practicable. The reduction in social distancing from 2m to 1m has allowed NICTS to implement a number of practical steps to facilitate significantly increased courtroom capacity numbers. However, it does not facilitate a resumption of Jury trials at Londonderry Courthouse or Downpatrick Courthouse where social distancing of 1m is not possible, nor is it possible to install protective screens.These arrangements will continue to be kept under review, as NICTS continues to monitor the prevailing advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps she is taking to address the NI Supply Teacher Register's payment and recruitment of temporary teachers.
answer_text: My Department does not employ teachers.  The Education Authority (EA) administers the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) on behalf of my Department.  All grant-aided schools can utilise the system to offer bookings to available registered teachers and latterly approve if the teacher accepts and attends.  All bookings approved prior to payroll cut-off dates are transferred in a file from the NISTR to the Department for processing of salary.A short delay in the go-live date of the new NISTR system from 22 to 29 August and performance issues experienced in the first few days of operation resulted in schools being unable to book teachers for August days on the new system in the normal manner.  The EA put extensive additional resources in place to manually input thousands of retrospective bookings on schools’ behalf and to provide assistance to those experiencing difficulties in using the new solution. The Department also extended the August payroll deadline by two days to provide additional time for bookings to be placed, accepted and approved on the system. Nearly 3,500 temporary teachers received salary payment on the normal payroll date (16 September) however it is regrettable that a number of teachers who worked in August did not receive payment for various reasons for example bookings that were not accepted or signed-off before payroll cut-off, technical access issues, non-provision of bank details and duplicate bookings.  Every potential avenue was explored to provide those affected with an interim payment option, however given the range and complexity of issues, no viable option could be identified.  The impacted teachers will receive payment as part of the next monthly payroll run. The NISTR Support Team and the system supplier are working diligently to resolve any known issues and to make ongoing improvements in line with feedback received from users.  Several fixes and additional functionality have already been actioned such as search by teacher name and resolution of log-in issues.  Additional targeted training is planned and supplementary training videos, Quick Reference Guides and FAQs are constantly being updated.When fully developed the new system will provide the envisaged intuitive, up-to-date booking system that enables schools to make contact with available, pre-employment checked supply teachers with ease and provides a platform for equitable employment opportunities for this vital part of our workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department have put in place to raise the awareness of oesophageal cancer.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of improving the awareness of oesophageal cancer and the need to promote early intervention. Information on oesophageal cancer, including its signs and symptoms, has been included on the Public Health Agency’s 'Be Cancer Aware’ website.In addition, in 2020 my Department provided discretionary support funding to OGCancerNI, a charity formed to benefit patients and families affected by oesophageal and gastric cancer.  The funding provided was used by the organisation to assist with vital awareness raising work during the COVID-19 pandemic.In June 2022, I was delighted to be invited by OGCancerNI to launch a mobile unit they have established to promote their “Catch it Early” oesophageal cancer awareness campaign and this will be used to enable face-to-face contact with people and organisations throughout Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average first appointment waiting time for red flagged patients with signs of oesophageal cancer in each Trust area.
To ask the Minister of Health what funding and research has been put in place to increase early detection and treatment for oesophageal cancer.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of improving the awareness of oesophageal cancer and the need to promote early intervention. Information on oesophageal cancer, including its signs and symptoms, has been included on the Public Health Agency’s 'Be Cancer Aware’ website.In addition, in 2020 my Department provided discretionary support funding to OGCancerNI, a charity formed to benefit patients and families affected by oesophageal and gastric cancer.  The funding provided was used by the organisation to assist with vital awareness raising work during the COVID-19 pandemic.In June 2022, I was delighted to be invited by OGCancerNI to launch a mobile unit they have established to promote their “Catch it Early” oesophageal cancer awareness campaign and this will be used to enable face-to-face contact with people and organisations throughout Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the rollout of the post operative cataract care scheme.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the process by which those on universal credit can access Health Service Sight Tests; and (ii) why this is not an automatic entitlement.
answer_text: A person who makes a claim to Universal Credit may be eligible for help with health service charges for themselves, their partner or their children through the Health Service Low Income Scheme; this includes help with costs for sight tests, glasses and contact lenses. Anyone wishing to make a claim for help with health service charges can download the form found on the nidirect website. The form must be printed off, completed and handed or posted into the local Jobs & Benefits office.  Forms can also be obtained from locations such as doctors’ surgeries and dental practices or their local Jobs & Benefits office. Eligible customers will receive either full or partial remission of health costs for a twelve-month period. Whilst there is no automatic entitlement to help with health costs under Universal Credit work is currently underway to update Universal Credit Service to include a direct link to the Health Care Costs Form (HC1).While the applications for Help with Health Costs are processed by the Department for Communities, it is the Department of Health (DoH) that owns the scheme and sets the criteria to determine who is entitled to the help.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of patients waiting for macular degeneration services in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) for an update on post-pandemic capacity of macular degeneration services.
answer_text: Services for patients with macular degeneration are provided for the region by Belfast and Western HSC Trusts. At the end of April 2022, there were 536 patients waiting for a first outpatient appointment in Belfast Trust and 98 patients waiting for a first outpatient appointment in Western Trust.As with most other areas, the capacity of the macular services provided in both Trusts was impacted significantly during the pandemic. Whilst the position has improved over recent months, there remain challenges associated with social distancing requirements in the current accommodation in both Trusts. This continues to impact on the capacity available. In line with commitments contained in the Elective Care Framework, additional capacity has been secured internally via megaclinics delivered by Western HSC Trust, and by utilisation of the Independent Sector to support HSC service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the staff shortages in (i) physiotherapy; (ii) speech and language therapy; and (iii) occupational therapy, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what support is being provided by his Department to support Derry’s Clipper Festival this summer.
answer_text: Tourism NI is providing £110,000 funding support to Foyle Maritime Festival 2022 under the International Tourism Events Funding Programme. The Festival includes the arrival and stopover of the Clipper Round the World Yacht Race. Marketing and promotional support is also being provided by Tourism NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to (i) outline what steps have been taken to disband the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland; and (ii) provide a timeline for this process.
answer_text: In my statement of 13 December 2021 to the Assembly, I committed to consult with the public, the teaching profession and key educational stakeholders on those functions currently assigned to GTCNI which are critical to the sector and must be preserved.The consultation on this important issue for all our teachers launched on 13 June 2022 and will run until 30 September 2022.  This extended consultation period should ensure all teachers have ample opportunity to share their views.Following the consultation, officials will need to reflect on the responses before developing firm Departmental recommendations for change and seeking Executive approval to legislate on this basis.Only after this can officials begin to prepare a draft Bill; and this Bill will be subject to a further public consultation exercise prior to its introduction to the Assembly.While this work remains a priority for the Department, we do not anticipate the introduction of this Bill before September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the future plans for the Engage II Programme.
answer_text: The Engage programme has been extremely successful to date.  With more than £24 million provided to 1,700 educational settings in the 2021/22 academic year, Engage has ensured that our children and young people have been provided with a range of support to reduce the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on their educational experience and learning. I issued a Direction to my officials for the Engage programme to continue to December 2022.  The Department of Finance approved that Direction on 6 July 2022, and I am delighted that I have been able to allocate £10million to continue the programme until the end of December 2022.   I have recently written to schools and settings to inform them.It is my intention that the programme will continue until the end of the academic year. However, as this remains subject to the necessary funding being made available, settings are not able to commit to expenditure beyond December 2022.  I will update schools on the budget position for the rest of the academic year as soon as I am in a position to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a timeline for the feasibility report and business case for the replacement of classrooms at the Gaelscoil Na Daróige in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority anticipate that the Feasibility Report for the replacement classrooms will be completed mid-August 2022 and the Business Case will be submitted to my Department for approval mid-October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what progress has been made to include Derry and the North West in the Wild Atlantic Way tourist trail.
answer_text: It was clear for many years that if Northern Ireland was to compete effectively for tourism spend, it urgently required its own distinctive tourism brand, which would be reflective of the experience that a visitor truly encounters when on a trip to Northern Ireland.The initial priority was to create a brand that would help the whole of Northern Ireland to stand out in the marketplace and ensure that this new brand was embedded with both the local industry and the international trade.Following extensive national and international testing, a new Experience Brand was developed and launched in 2019: Northern Ireland – Embrace a Giant Spirit.  When this brand was tested against existing marketing content, there was a 30% increase in the number of people showing an interest in visiting Northern Ireland. This potential increase in visitors would contribute significantly to the overall longer-term objective of growing our tourism sector to a level that will deliver a two billion pounds benefit to the Northern Ireland economy.The Causeway Coastal Route and Walled City of Londonderry are two key offers within the new brand and several brand demonstrators have been identified, supported and showcased in each area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what programmes are being adopted by Policing and Community Safety Partnerships to address homophobia and transphobia.
answer_text: Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) are independent statutory bodies established by the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. They are funded jointly by the Department of Justice and the Northern Ireland Policing Board in each of the eleven Council areas.  Each PCSP is made up of elected, independent and statutory members who agree on the appropriate allocation of funding towards programmes that prioritise local community safety concerns within their areas.  They produce annual action plans to deliver practical solutions to address these issues. In addition, they publish annually an effectiveness report to show how they have delivered on the programmes. PCSP action plans for 2022/23 include a range of programmes and initiatives to help make communities safer and tackle all types of crime including transphobic and homophobic hate incidents and crime. Initiatives are in place to raise awareness, prevent crimes, improve victim support and provide information on how hate crimes can be reported to the PSNI.   Programmes running across a number of the PCSP areas include:security based programmes which raise awareness and promote crime prevention and home /personal safety through delivery of the 4 Tier Security Scheme and the supply and fitting of home security equipment;a focus on raising awareness of hate crime and increase awareness of the support available to victims, including transphobic and homophobic victims; andinteractive training on bias language and stereotypes to support participants to speak out against hate crime in their local community.Details of all PCSP programmes and initiatives are made available by each PCSP on their website and social media platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of how many transphobic hate crimes and incidents have been reported to the PSNI in each year, between 2010 and 2022.
answer_text: The recording of information on offences involving hate crime is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on Incidents and Crimes with a hate motivation that are recorded by them. You may, therefore, wish to direct any question relating to the number of recorded offences involving hate crime to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many children and young people in Foyle are waiting for their first appointment with Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services.
answer_text: My Department does not collect data on the number of children waiting to access Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) by constituency, and to obtain this information would be at a disproportionate cost. However, we do receive the figures by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust; at 31 May 2022, there were 538 children and young people waiting to access CAMHS in Western HSC Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of how many homophobic hate crimes and incidents have been reported to the PSNI in each year, between 2010 and 2022.
answer_text: The recording of information on offences involving hate crime is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on Incidents and Crimes with a hate motivation that are recorded by them. You may, therefore, wish to direct any question relating to the number of recorded offences involving hate crime to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps were taken to assess the environmental and health impacts that gold mining would have in Foyle.
answer_text: The Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969, vests most minerals in my Department with a number of exceptions, the most notable being gold and silver. As gold remains the property of the Crown Estate Commissioners, my Department has no role in the licensing of gold extraction in Northern Ireland.The environmental and health impacts of gold mining in Northern Ireland are a matter for the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department of Health, respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps were taken to engage with local residents and stakeholders in Foyle, prior to the awarding of gold mining contracts.
answer_text: The Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969, vests all minerals in my Department with a number of exceptions, the most notable being gold and silver. As gold remains the property of the Crown Estate Commissioners, my Department has no role in the licensing of gold extraction in Northern Ireland.The engagement with residents and stakeholders prior to the awarding of gold mining contracts in Foyle is a matter for the Crown Estate Commissioners

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps have been taken to update the current framework for awarding gold mining contracts.
answer_text: The Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969, vests most minerals in my Department with a number of exceptions, the most notable being gold and silver. As gold remains the property of the Crown Estate Commissioners, my Department has no role in the licensing of gold extraction in Northern Ireland.Therefore, the awarding of gold mining contracts and the updating of any frameworks is a matter for the Crown Estate Commissioners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many staff vacancies currently exist in (i) occupational therapy; (ii) physiotherapy; (iii) speech and language therapy; and (iv) psychology.
answer_text: Information on the number of occupational therapist, physiotherapist, speech & language therapist, and clinical psychologist vacancies actively being recruited to at 31 March 2022 is detailed in the table below.ProfessionVacancies actively being recruited to at 31 March 2022Occupational Therapist116Physiotherapist186Speech & Language Therapist50Clinical Psychologist90Source: Health and Social Care (HSC) organisationsThis information is published on a quarterly basis on the Department of Health website, and can be found at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/staff-vacanciesFor 2022/23, I have increased the number of pre-registration training places, on programmes commissioned by my Department from Ulster University, as follows:Occupational therapy – increased by 10 up to a total of 65 training placesPhysiotherapy – increased by 10 up to a total of 90 training placesSpeech and language therapy – increased by 5 up to a total of 33 training placesI can also advise that at 31 March 2022 there were 1,211.7 WTE occupational therapists, 1,182.2 WTE physiotherapists, 574.0 WTE speech and language therapists, and 384.1 WTE clinical psychologists employed across HSC.  This represented an increase across all four professions from the previous March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of adult respite and emergency respite beds; (ii) the number of people currently on waiting lists for respite services; (iii) the length of time they have been waiting; and (iv) his plans for providing adequate levels of respite care.
answer_text: Covid-19 has impacted the availability of respite beds across the region and a number of care home providers have withdrawn respite care. The Public Health Agency have provided advice to further increase respite bed provision, which will require adjustment to testing policy in care homes.Further details on service provision and remobilisation can be found on the Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-services In addition, the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group will monitor remobilisation across Trusts and address regional disparity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the number of adults who are waiting for psychology assessment and for how long they have been waiting.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect the number of adults waiting for a psychology assessment; therefore, the data was requested from each Trust.  As at 31 May 2022, 4,379 adults were waiting for a psychology assessment, as follows:HSC Trust0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks9-13 weeks13 + weeksTotalBelfast45916046626868Northern1161181071217121,174South Eastern36394056565736Southern53394761628828Western594452665527733093313063503,0834,379Source: HSC TrustsThe Northern Trust’s data suggests longer waiting times compared to other Trusts. This is because psychologists at the Northern Trust work within multiple multi-disciplinary teams and waiting lists are held for the whole service, rather than separate waiting lists for specific professions such as psychology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the number of children who are waiting for a psychology assessment and for how long they have been waiting.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect the number of children and young people waiting for a psychology assessment; therefore, these figures were requested from each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust. As a result, information in regards to length of time waiting for a psychology assessment has been supplied in differing formats as set out below.
