question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department is taking to address homophobic and transphobic bullying in schools.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools (NI) Act 2016, which came into effect from 01 September 2021, requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying, their motivation and their outcome, including bullying in relation to sexual orientation.  Following the introduction of Act, an update resource ‘Effective Responses to Bullying Behaviour’ was published to provide schools with practical advice for responding to a bullying incident.  This includes how capturing the motivation behind any specific problems will help identify if any homophobic and transphobic bullying has occurred and allow the school to put in place suitable protocols and educational programmes for dealing with those issues. My Department currently provides funding to the Northern Ireland Anti-Bullying Forum (NIABF), which is an independent body comprising 25 statutory and non-statutory organisations involved directly or indirectly in the reduction of all forms of bullying and creating a safe environment for children and young people.  Where a school identifies a particular problem, such as homophobic and transphobic bullying, the NIABF co-ordinator will signpost them to Forum member organisations who can provide expert advice. My Department has also provided funding to the Education Authority (EA) to establish a dedicated team of permanent specialist staff to support schools, educational settings and young people to further embed cultures that eradicate all types of bullying for young people across educational settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department is taking to ensure schools are safe and welcoming environments for LGBTQIA+ students.
answer_text: It is important that schools are safe places where children and young people can express themselves in an open manner and are taught to respect themselves and others.The governors and Principal of a school should maintain an ethos for the school that promotes the moral, spiritual, intellectual, social and personal development of all its pupils.  The school’s ethos should contribute to the wider goals of the school and be clearly defined and understood by parents, pupils, staff, governors and the local community.  It should also be consistent with a commitment to promote equality, good relations and diversity within the school and its community and safeguarding and promoting the welfare and protection of pupils.	The ethos, policies and practices of a school should value all young people. Education for all children should be provided in an environment that is positive and welcoming.One of the roles of the Board of Governors is to ensure that the school’s pastoral care arrangements protect the pupils from harm, safeguard their health and welfare and support their learning and development.  It should be noted that the Board of Governors has a statutory duty to:safeguard and promote the welfare of registered pupils at the school; decide the measures to be taken to protect pupils from abuse; and have measures in place within its discipline policies to prevent bullying.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps are being taken to retain Irish-medium teaching and non-teaching staff.
answer_text: “A Fair Start” recommended that additional, focused support should be provided for Irish-medium education in relation to Teacher Professional Learning (TPL), leadership training, resources and Initial Teacher Education (ITE).  My officials are working with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) on implementing this recommendation and my Department provided funding in the 2021/22 Financial Year to support focused research, to result in specific recommendations about addressing teacher supply to meet need. I expect this, among other matters, to include recommendations about the retention of beginning teachers, the potential to identify teaching and non-teaching staff not currently working in Irish-medium schools and consider support training to enable them to move across sectors, as well as make best use of the Learning Leaders Support Scheme which exists to support ongoing professional development for both teaching and non-teaching staff.  Once received, my officials will continue to work with CnaG and other partner bodies, including the Education Authority and ITE providers, to consider any such recommendations.  I have approved further funding support to underpin A Fair Start in 2022/23 which will enable this work to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps are being taken to create additional Irish Medium teacher training places given the shortage of trained professionals.
answer_text: I am aware of the concerns of the Irish-medium sector about the availability of suitably qualified teachers.  My Department works alongside the Department for the Economy to determine the number of students admitted each year to Initial Teacher Education (ITE) courses in Northern Ireland.  Intake numbers are based on a number of relevant factors, including the predicted future need for teachers across the Northern Ireland education system and including specific consideration of the needs of the Irish-medium sector.“A Fair Start” included a recommendation that additional, focused support should be provided for Irish-medium ITE, among other matters.  My officials are working with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) on this recommendation and my Department provided funding in the 2021/22 Financial Year to support focused research, to result in specific recommendations about addressing teacher supply to meet need.  I expect this, among other matters, to include recommendations about reviewing the approach to the post-primary PGCE course for Irish-medium education and my officials will continue to work with CnaG and other partner bodies to consider any such recommendations.  I have approved further funding support to underpin A Fair Start in 2022/23 which will enable this work to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many car parking fines were issued at Western Health and Social Care Trust facilities between January 2022 and May 2022.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Western Health & Social Care Trust has confirmed that a total of 907 car parking fines were issued at Trust facilities, between 1st January 2022 and 31st May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what steps her Department is taking to address transphobic abuse.
answer_text: Hate crime in any form, including transphobic abuse is unacceptable and can lead to attitudes and behaviours that lead to criminal offences.I am aware from the most recent hate crime statistics, published by PSNI in May 2022 that the number of transphobic hate crimes and incidents has increased and reached a record high since the recording of transphobic hate motivations began in 2009/10.  Addressing hate crime is one of my priorities. This requires a collaborative response with partners in relation to tackling enabling factors of intolerance, prejudice and hate, as well as a robust legislative response to ensure action is taken against offenders, where necessary, and to increase victims’ confidence in the system that brings offenders to justice.In supporting victims of hate crime, my Department in conjunction with the PSNI continues to fund the Hate Crime Advocacy Service. The Service is provided on a consortium basis led by Victim Support NI. It includes an advocate from The Rainbow Project who can support victims of transphobic hate crime through the criminal justice process and sign post victims to a range of other support organisations and servicesIn response to Judge Marrinan’s recommendations on strengthening hate crime legislation, the development of a range of policies is ongoing with the intention of introducing a Hate Crime Bill in this mandate.   In addition to maintaining the current four protected characteristics (race, religion, disability and sexual orientation) in hate crime law, Judge Marrinan recommended extending protection to sex/gender (gender neutral and including gender identity) and variations in sex characteristics (including transgender identity and intersex).  I have indicated in the Departmental Response to the recommendations, that I will include transgender identity as a protected category within the draft legislation. This Bill will improve the criminal justice process for victims of hate crime, including transgender hate crime victims; help inform rehabilitative work and act as a deterrent for potential perpetrators; and reduce the risk of reoffending and further victimisation. I am also working with the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) to ensure the recommendations on helping victims of online hate abuse and crime are considered during the development of the Online Safety Bill which has been introduced at Westminster. On an operational level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), which are jointly funded by the Department of Justice and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, have a role in addressing hate crime, and the fear of hate crime through specific local Hate Crime projects.  As part of their initiatives for 2022/23, a range of  PCSPs are developing initiatives to raise awareness, prevent crimes, improve victim support and provide information on how hate crimes, including transphobic hate crime, can be reported to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps have been taken to increase car parking capacity at the emergency department of Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Western Health & Social Care Trust has confirmed that there has not been any increase in car parking capacity at the Emergency Department of Altnagelvin Hospital. However, the overall car parking capacity on the Altnagelvin site, has been increased by 100 spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many hospital car parking fines were issued at Altnagelvin Hospital between January 2022 and May 2022.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Western Health & Social Care Trust has confirmed that 469 car parking fines were issued at Altnagelvin Hospital between 1st January 2022 and 31st May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the steps her Department has taken to ensure Irish Medium schools can access extra-curricular activities through the target language.
answer_text: The Extended Schools (ES) programme, which has been in place since 2006, aims to improve educational outcomes through the provision of additional activities and services which seek to reduce barriers to learning and provide the necessary support to children and young people. Including extra learning support for languages. A total of £9.153 million of ES funding has been provided in the 2021/22 and 2022/23 financial years to schools.Since 2012/13, an additional strand was added to enhance the use of the Irish language across schools and communities (the “Irish Language programme”). £275,000 of the £9.153 million is allocated each year to the Irish language strand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the steps her Department has taken to provide fully translated policy documents to Irish Medium schools.
answer_text: On 5 September 2016 the Minister of Education approved a new language policy for the Department.The policy recognises the Department’s statutory duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish medium education and responsibility to meet the learning needs whose language of instruction is Irish.  The policy requires information such as leaflets for pupils and inspection reports translated and issued to Irish medium schools and units in Irish.  Requests for the translation of other documents into Irish will be considered on an individual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what steps her Department is taking to address homophobic abuse.
answer_text: Hate in any form, including homophobic abuse, is unacceptable and can lead to attitudes and behaviours that lead to criminal offences. I am aware from the most recent hate crime statistics, published by PSNI in May 2022, that the number of homophobic hate crimes and incidents has reached a record high since the recording of hate motivations began in 2004/05.  Addressing hate crime is one of my priorities. This requires a collaborative response with partners in relation to tackling enabling factors of intolerance, prejudice and hate, as well as a robust legislative response to ensure action is taken against offenders, where necessary, and to increase victims’ confidence in the system that brings offenders to justice.My Department, in conjunction with the PSNI, funds the Hate Crime Advocacy Service in order to support victims of hate crimes. The Service is provided on a consortium basis led by Victim Support NI. It includes an advocate from The Rainbow Project who can support victims of homophobic hate crime through the criminal justice process and sign post victims to a range of other support organisations and services.  In response to Judge Marrinan’s recommendations on strengthening hate crime legislation, the development of a range of policies is ongoing, with the intention of introducing a Hate Crime Bill in this mandate. This Bill will improve the criminal justice process for victims of hate crime, including homophobic hate crime victims; help inform rehabilitative work and act as a deterrent for potential perpetrators; and reduce the risk of reoffending and further victimisation. I am also working with the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) to ensure the recommendations on helping victims of online hate abuse and crime are considered during the development of the Online Safety Bill which has been introduced at Westminster. On an operational level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), which are jointly funded by the Department of Justice and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, have a role in addressing hate crime and the fear of hate crime through local projects.  For example, Derry & Strabane PCSP, as part of their initiatives for 2022/23, are developing a programme with a focus on raising awareness of hate crime and support available for victims, which will include homophobia.  On a practical level, the PCSP is also supplying and fitting home security equipment for residents who have been victims of crime or experiencing fear of crime, including hate crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the need for capital builds for Irish Medium schools in Derry.
answer_text: When a call for Major Capital Works is announced it is the responsibility of the Managing Authorities to submit schools to be considered for major works.  In the case of Irish Medium schools it is the responsibility of Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG). Each school is then assessed against the Protocol for Selection of Major Capital Works. The approach adopted in the protocol is designed to ensure that all project proposals are considered fairly and consistently against stated selection criteria.Gaelscoil Úi Dhochartaigh was announced to proceed to design in January 2013 with an approved Business Case of £4.8m.  The project for a 7 class primary school and single nursery unit is due to commence in August 2022.	New build projects are only one avenue of my Department’s Capital Investment programme, and further investment is delivered through the School Enhancement and the Minor Works programmes. Capital projects are currently planned or have been recently completed at three Irish Medium schools in the Derry City & Strabane Council area.At Gaelscoil Eadain Mhoir, additional accommodation and repairs to the existing premises were completed in 2019/20 at an approved construction cost of £269k.Further work to erect safeguarding fencing was completed in 2020/21 at a cost of £11k.		At Bunscoil Cholmcille, a number of minor works projects have been proposed to address existing needs within the school, including the provision of CCTV, a multi-purpose hall with school meals servery and new toilet provision.Recently completed minor works include resurfacing completed in 2021/22 and the installation of safeguarding fencing in 2020/21 at a combined cost of approximately £50k.Gaelscoil na Daróige has seen substantial capital investment in relation to minor works. In November 2021 a scheme to deliver dining accommodation and an additional classroom at the school was completed at a cost of approximately £215k.Furthermore, in June 2021, the Department purchased privately owned land adjacent to the school on the Cosquin Road valued at £210,000. The Department is also is the process of finalising the purchase of lands owned by the NIHE to the value of £45k. The purchase of these additional lands will allow for future expansion at the school.Currently, the Education Authority is in the process of completing a Feasibility Report and Business Case for the replacement of classrooms at the school. In addition, a Business Case was approved in February 2022 for a gate entry system at a cost of £15.7k.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps her Department is taking to promote enrolment into Irish Medium education in Derry.
answer_text: My Department takes very seriously its duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-Medium (IM) education; however, my Department does not promote enrolment into any school or sector.  Rather it strives to ensure that, as far as possible, children are educated in accordance with the wishes of their parents. My Department funds Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) to encourage and promote IM education and CnaG works within the Area Planning structures to reflect demand for places in the IM sector in every geographical locale.In delivering its duty to encourage and facilitate the development of IM education, my Department has also provided significant capital investment in IM education in Londonderry as detailed in my reply to AQW 982/22-27. The Education Authority (EA), is the statutory planning authority and has overall operational responsibility for planning education provision.  All Area Planning stakeholders (including CnaG) have worked together to produce the next Strategic Area Plan (SAP) which will come to me for endorsement in the very near future.The Schools for the Future: A Policy for Sustainable Schools (SSP) User Guide has been developed to take account of changes since the SSP’s introduction. Section 5 of the guidance relates to ‘Developing Schools’ (new schools) and the Department’s expectation that a new school will grow to become educationally and financially sustainable within a reasonable timeframe of 10 years from the date of opening.  The guidance details the key milestones in a new school’s development with mitigating actions to be taken if milestones are not met.  The timeframe for some IM and Integrated schools can be extended by a further five years (making a total period of 15 years) to allow the schools to become educationally and financially sustainable.  The SSP User Guide can be accessed using the following link:Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) User Guide - February 2022 - Final.pdf (education-ni.gov.uk)I hope this information is helping in explaining my Department’s position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions his Department is taking to attract students and increase the number of optometrist training places in local universities.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s universities are autonomous institutions and as such are responsible for their own course provision and student recruitment.While my Department sets a Maximum Student Number (MaSN) cap for each institution, it is up to the institutions to decide for themselves how many places they allocate to specific subjects and courses. The Northern Ireland Skills Barometer indicates that, while Higher Education skills are only marginally undersupplied, there is still an imbalance across individual subject areas in terms of meeting labour market demand. It is therefore important to ensure that HE provision is better aligned with economic need. To address this, consideration is currently being given to how Outcome Agreements, funding arrangements and other options could potentially be used to better align educational output with the needs of the local economy. As per the Department’s Skills Strategy, my Department is primarily focused on increasing the number of students studying economically relevant STEM subjects and developing digital skills. The Skills Barometer indicates that Optometry is an area which is not significantly undersupplied by local higher level skills. The Department is not in a position to provide financial incentives to employers to encourage them to employ Optometry graduates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to address the waiting lists for cataract surgery.
answer_text: In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists.  The pandemic has had a negative impact on waiting times for many elective care services including for cataract surgery.   Strenuous efforts are being made across the Health and Social Care (HSC) System to reduce the current long waiting times, including rebuilding activity in cataract day procedure centres and securing additional capacity by working with independent sector providers. I am pleased to note that these efforts are having a positive effect, with waiting lists and times for both routine and urgent cataract treatments starting to reduce. We have established robust monitoring arrangements to ensure that non-recurrent funding made available for elective waiting lists is utilised to see and treat as many patients as possible.   This non-recurrent funding is additional to core funding for HSC baseline delivery for cataract treatments. It is available for both in-house HSC-delivered care, and to fund additional capacity from the Independent Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to reduce the waiting times for audiology appointments.
answer_text: In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework setting out 55 actions to reduce waiting times.  The Framework highlights the need for additional capacity in order to reduce waiting lists. The Framework sets out a five year plan with firm, time bound proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future.Last month I announced the allocation of £46m of non-recurrent funding in the first six months of 2022/23 for Waiting List Initiatives (WLI) to help reduce waiting times for a range of elective care services, including diagnostic tests. This funding is being used to secure additional capacity across a range of elective services including audiology. HSC Trusts have bid £137k in total to deliver additional audiology services through WLI in Quarter 1 and Quarter 2 of 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to attract students and increase the number of optometrist training places in local universities.
answer_text: The Department is working with the local optometry training provider, Ulster University, to ensure the course remains attractive and strategically fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all COVID restrictions have been relaxed in maternity services and settings.
answer_text: I am very aware of the impact that visiting restrictions have had on women and their birth partners throughout the COVID-19 pandemic. While many of the restrictions in society intended to provide protection from the risk of COVID-19 transmission have been significantly eased, there still remains a need to protect our most vulnerable, including pregnant women.  Visiting in Maternity services continues to be facilitated in line with the arrangements in the Pathway to Enhanced Visiting, available on the Department’s website here. This guidance represents the expected visiting arrangements which should be facilitated, providing that such decisions are guided by local Infection Prevention and Control risk assessments.  In the main, restrictions are limited as follows:Maternity Outpatient Services: A chosen birth partner will be facilitated to accompany the pregnant woman to any pregnancy related appointments and ultrasound scans with particular effort made to keep the environment COVID-19 secure.Labour and Birth: A chosen birth partner will be facilitated to accompany the pregnant woman for induction of labour, duration of labour and birth and, for up to three hours after the birth.Maternity Inpatient Services: One daily visit from one of two nominated individuals (from up to two households) can be permitted, with particular effort made to keep the environment COVID-19 secure. Where the visitor requires assistance then no more than 1 additional person will be permitted to accompany them when this can be accommodated within social distancing guidance. Duration of visits should be agreed with person in charge. Alternatives, for example, outdoor visiting, virtual visits, should continue to be made available.From time to time local decisions may have to be made to reinstate some additional restrictions for limited periods to protect patients, families and staff.  These arrangements are kept under regular review, so the most up to date information on visiting across specific services and settings is published on each individual Health & Social Care Trust’s website.
